(**DRC**),[[b]](#cite_note-8) also known as the **DR Congo**, **Congo-Kinshasa**, or simply the **Congo**[[c]](#cite_note-9) or less often **Zaire**,[[d]](#cite_note-10) is a country in [Central Africa](/wiki/Central_Africa "Central Africa"). By land area, it is the [second-largest country in Africa](/wiki/List_of_African_countries_by_area "List of African countries by area") and the [11th-largest in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_area "List of countries and dependencies by area"). With a population of around 124 million people, the Democratic Republic of the Congo is the [fourth-most populous](/wiki/List_of_African_countries_by_population "List of African countries by population") in Africa and most populous [Francophone country](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_French_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where French is an official language") in the world. [French](/wiki/Belgian_French "Belgian French") is the official and most widely spoken language, though there are [over 200 indigenous languages](/wiki/Languages_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Languages of the Democratic Republic of the Congo"), of which [Lingala](/wiki/Lingala "Lingala") is the most widely spoken. The capital, largest city, and economic center is [Kinshasa](/wiki/Kinshasa "Kinshasa"). The DRC is bordered by the [Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Republic_of_the_Congo "Republic of the Congo"), the [Cabinda exclave of Angola](/wiki/Cabinda_Province "Cabinda Province"), and the [South Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/South_Atlantic_Ocean "South Atlantic Ocean") to the west; the [Central African Republic](/wiki/Central_African_Republic "Central African Republic") and [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan") to the north; [Uganda](/wiki/Uganda "Uganda"), [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda "Rwanda"), [Burundi](/wiki/Burundi "Burundi"), and [Tanzania](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania") (across [Lake Tanganyika](/wiki/Lake_Tanganyika "Lake Tanganyika")) to the east; and [Zambia](/wiki/Zambia "Zambia") and [Angola](/wiki/Angola "Angola") to the south. Centered on the [Congo Basin](/wiki/Congo_Basin "Congo Basin"), most of the country's [terrain](/wiki/Geography_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Geography of the Democratic Republic of the Congo") is covered by [dense rainforests](/wiki/Congolian_rainforests "Congolian rainforests") and is crossed by many rivers, while the east and southeast are mountainous.

The **Democratic Republic of the Congo** (**DRC**),[[b]](#cite_note-8) also known as the **DR Congo**, **Congo-Kinshasa**, or simply the **Congo**[[c]](#cite_note-9) or less often **Zaire**,[[d]](#cite_note-10) is a country in [Central Africa](/wiki/Central_Africa "Central Africa"). By land area, it is the [second-largest country in Africa](/wiki/List_of_African_countries_by_area "List of African countries by area") and the [11th-largest in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_area "List of countries and dependencies by area"). With a population of around 124 million people, the Democratic Republic of the Congo is the [fourth-most populous](/wiki/List_of_African_countries_by_population "List of African countries by population") in Africa and most populous [Francophone country](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_French_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where French is an official language") in the world. [French](/wiki/Belgian_French "Belgian French") is the official and most widely spoken language, though there are [over 200 indigenous languages](/wiki/Languages_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Languages of the Democratic Republic of the Congo"), of which [Lingala](/wiki/Lingala "Lingala") is the most widely spoken. The capital, largest city, and economic center is [Kinshasa](/wiki/Kinshasa "Kinshasa"). The DRC is bordered by the [Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Republic_of_the_Congo "Republic of the Congo"), the [Cabinda exclave of Angola](/wiki/Cabinda_Province "Cabinda Province"), and the [South Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/South_Atlantic_Ocean "South Atlantic Ocean") to the west; the [Central African Republic](/wiki/Central_African_Republic "Central African Republic") and [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan") to the north; [Uganda](/wiki/Uganda "Uganda"), [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda "Rwanda"), [Burundi](/wiki/Burundi "Burundi"), and [Tanzania](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania") (across [Lake Tanganyika](/wiki/Lake_Tanganyika "Lake Tanganyika")) to the east; and [Zambia](/wiki/Zambia "Zambia") and [Angola](/wiki/Angola "Angola") to the south. Centered on the [Congo Basin](/wiki/Congo_Basin "Congo Basin"), most of the country's [terrain](/wiki/Geography_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Geography of the Democratic Republic of the Congo") is covered by [dense rainforests](/wiki/Congolian_rainforests "Congolian rainforests") and is crossed by many rivers, while the east and southeast are mountainous.

The territory of the Congo was first inhabited by [Central African foragers](/wiki/African_Pygmies "African Pygmies") around 90,000 years ago and was settled in the [Bantu expansion](/wiki/Bantu_expansion "Bantu expansion") about 2,000 to 3,000 years ago.[[7]](#cite_note-11) In the west, the [Kingdom of Kongo](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kongo "Kingdom of Kongo") ruled around the mouth of the [Congo River](/wiki/Congo_River "Congo River") from the 14th to the 19th century. In the center and east, the empires of [Mwene Muji](/wiki/Mwene_Muji "Mwene Muji"), [Luba](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Luba "Kingdom of Luba"), and [Lunda](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Lunda "Kingdom of Lunda") ruled between the 15th and 19th centuries. These kingdoms were broken up by Europeans during the [colonization of the Congo Basin](/wiki/Colonization_of_the_Congo_Basin "Colonization of the Congo Basin"). King [Leopold II of Belgium](/wiki/Leopold_II_of_Belgium "Leopold II of Belgium") acquired rights to the Congo territory in 1885 and called it the [Congo Free State](/wiki/Congo_Free_State "Congo Free State"). In 1908, Leopold [ceded](/wiki/Leopold_II_of_Belgium#Relinquishment_of_the_Congo "Leopold II of Belgium") the territory after international pressure in response to [widespread atrocities](/wiki/Atrocities_in_the_Congo_Free_State "Atrocities in the Congo Free State"), and it became a [Belgian colony](/wiki/Belgian_Congo "Belgian Congo"). Congo achieved [independence](/wiki/Republic_of_the_Congo_(L%C3%A9opoldville) "Republic of the Congo (Léopoldville)") from [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") in 1960 and was immediately confronted by [secessionist movements](/wiki/Congo_Crisis "Congo Crisis"), the assassination of Prime Minister [Patrice Lumumba](/wiki/Patrice_Lumumba "Patrice Lumumba"), and the [seizure of power](/wiki/1965_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1965 Democratic Republic of the Congo coup d'état") by [Mobutu Sese Seko](/wiki/Mobutu_Sese_Seko "Mobutu Sese Seko") in 1965. Mobutu renamed the country [Zaire](/wiki/Zaire "Zaire") in 1971 and imposed a [personalist dictatorship](/wiki/Personalist_dictatorship "Personalist dictatorship").[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECIA_World_Factbook_I"Introduction"-12)

Instability caused by the influx of refugees from the [Rwandan Civil War](/wiki/Rwandan_Civil_War "Rwandan Civil War") into the east of the country led to the [First Congo War](/wiki/First_Congo_War "First Congo War") between 1996 and 1997, ending in Mobutu's overthrow.[[9]](#cite_note-Luneghe_GPJ_I_Will_Not_Leave_This_Place-13) Its name was changed back to the DRC and it was confronted by the [Second Congo War](/wiki/Second_Congo_War "Second Congo War") from 1998 to 2003, which resulted in an estimated several million deaths and the assassination of President [Laurent-Désiré Kabila](/wiki/Laurent-D%C3%A9sir%C3%A9_Kabila "Laurent-Désiré Kabila").[[10]](#cite_note-IRC2007fullreport-14)[[11]](#cite_note-The_deadliest_war_in_the_world-15)[[12]](#cite_note-16)[[13]](#cite_note-IRC2007Facts-17) The war ended under President [Joseph Kabila](/wiki/Joseph_Kabila "Joseph Kabila"), who restored relative stability, though fighting continued in the east.[[14]](#cite_note-CFR2025-18) [Human rights](/wiki/Human_rights_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Human rights in the Democratic Republic of the Congo") remained poor, and there were frequent abuses, such as [forced disappearances](/wiki/Enforced_disappearance "Enforced disappearance"), torture, arbitrary imprisonment and restrictions on civil liberties.[[15]](#cite_note-:0-19) Kabila stepped down in 2019, the country's first [peaceful transition of power](/wiki/Peaceful_transition_of_power "Peaceful transition of power") since independence, after [Félix Tshisekedi](/wiki/F%C3%A9lix_Tshisekedi "Félix Tshisekedi") won the contentious [2018 general election](/wiki/2018_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_general_election "2018 Democratic Republic of the Congo general election").[[16]](#cite_note-20) Over [100 armed groups](/wiki/List_of_armed_groups_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "List of armed groups in the Democratic Republic of the Congo") remain active, [concentrated in the Kivu region](/wiki/Kivu_conflict "Kivu conflict"). One of the DRC's largest cities, [Goma](/wiki/Goma "Goma"), was occupied by the [March 23 Movement](/wiki/March_23_Movement "March 23 Movement") (M23) rebels [in 2012](/wiki/M23_rebellion_(2012%E2%80%932013) "M23 rebellion (2012–2013)") and [since 2025](/wiki/2025_Goma_offensive "2025 Goma offensive"). The [M23 uprising](/wiki/M23_campaign_(2022%E2%80%93present) "M23 campaign (2022–present)") escalated in early 2025, with military support from [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda "Rwanda"), which has caused a [conflict between the two countries](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo%E2%80%93Rwanda_conflict_(2022%E2%80%93present) "Democratic Republic of the Congo–Rwanda conflict (2022–present)").[[17]](#cite_note-21)[[18]](#cite_note-22)[[19]](#cite_note-Reuters1-23) A [peace agreement](/wiki/2025_DRC%E2%80%93Rwanda_peace_agreement "2025 DRC–Rwanda peace agreement") brokered by the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") was signed by Rwanda and the DRC in June 2025.[[20]](#cite_note-France24-27062025-24)

Despite being incredibly rich in [natural resources](/wiki/Natural_resource "Natural resource"), the DRC is one of the world's poorest countries, having suffered from political instability, lack of infrastructure, rampant corruption, centuries of commercial and colonial extraction and exploitation, with little widespread development.[[21]](#cite_note-:9-25)[[22]](#cite_note-26) The nation is a prominent example of the "[resource curse](/wiki/Resource_curse "Resource curse")".[[23]](#cite_note-27) Aside from Kinshasa, the next largest cities, [Lubumbashi](/wiki/Lubumbashi "Lubumbashi") and [Mbuji-Mayi](/wiki/Mbuji-Mayi "Mbuji-Mayi"), are [mining communities](/wiki/Mining_community "Mining community"). The largest exports are raw [minerals](/wiki/Mineral "Mineral") and [metal](/wiki/Metal "Metal"), which accounted for 80% of exports in 2023, with China being the DR Congo's largest trade partner.[[24]](#cite_note-UNCTAD-28)[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECIA_World_Factbook_I"Economy"-29) For 2023, DR Congo's level of human development was ranked 171 out of 193 countries by the [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index").[[6]](#cite_note-UNHDR-7) It is classified one of the [least developed countries](/wiki/Least_developed_countries "Least developed countries") by the UN.[[26]](#cite_note-30) As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit), following two decades of [civil wars](#Continental_and_civil_wars_(1996–2007)) and [internal conflicts](#Continued_conflicts_(2008–2018)), around one million Congolese refugees were still living in neighbouring countries.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECIA_World_Factbook_IPeople_and_Society-31) Two million children are at risk of starvation, and the fighting has displaced 7 million people.[[28]](#cite_note-32)[[29]](#cite_note-33) The country is a member of the UN, [Non-Aligned Movement](/wiki/Non-Aligned_Movement "Non-Aligned Movement"), [African Union](/wiki/African_Union "African Union"), [COMESA](/wiki/COMESA "COMESA"), [Southern African Development Community](/wiki/Southern_African_Development_Community "Southern African Development Community"), *[Organisation Internationale de la Francophonie](/wiki/Organisation_internationale_de_la_Francophonie "Organisation internationale de la Francophonie")*, and [Economic Community of Central African States](/wiki/Economic_Community_of_Central_African_States "Economic Community of Central African States").

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

Further information: [Congo River](/wiki/Congo_River "Congo River") and [Kongo people](/wiki/Kongo_people "Kongo people")

The Democratic Republic of the Congo is named after the [Congo River](/wiki/Congo_River "Congo River"), which flows through the country. The Congo River is the world's deepest river and the world's third-largest river [by discharge](/wiki/List_of_rivers_by_discharge "List of rivers by discharge"). The *Comité d'études du haut Congo* ("Committee for the Study of the Upper Congo"), established by King [Leopold II of Belgium](/wiki/Leopold_II_of_Belgium "Leopold II of Belgium") in 1876, and the [International Association of the Congo](/wiki/International_Association_of_the_Congo "International Association of the Congo"), established by him in 1879, were also named after the river.[[30]](#cite_note-34)

The Congo River was named by early European sailors after the [Kingdom of Kongo](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kongo "Kingdom of Kongo") and its [Bantu](/wiki/Bantu_peoples "Bantu peoples") inhabitants, the [Kongo people](/wiki/Kongo_people "Kongo people"), when they encountered them in the 16th century.[[31]](#cite_note-35)[[32]](#cite_note-36) The word *Kongo* comes from the [Kongo language](/wiki/Kongo_language "Kongo language") (also called *Kikongo*). According to American writer Samuel Henry Nelson: "It is probable that the word 'Kongo' itself implies a public gathering and that it is based on the root *konga*, 'to gather' (trans[itive])."[[33]](#cite_note-37) The modern name of the Kongo people, *Bakongo*, was introduced in the early 20th century.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Democratic Republic of the Congo has been known in the past as, in chronological order, the [Congo Free State](/wiki/Congo_Free_State "Congo Free State"), [Belgian Congo](/wiki/Belgian_Congo "Belgian Congo"), the [Republic of the Congo-Léopoldville](/wiki/Republic_of_the_Congo_(L%C3%A9opoldville) "Republic of the Congo (Léopoldville)"), the Democratic Republic of the Congo and the [Republic of Zaire](/wiki/Republic_of_Zaire "Republic of Zaire"), before returning to its current name, the Democratic Republic of the Congo.[[2]](#cite_note-cia.gov-3)

At the time of independence, the country was named the Republic of the Congo-Léopoldville to distinguish it from its neighbour [Congo](/wiki/Republic_of_the_Congo "Republic of the Congo"), officially the [Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Republic_of_the_Congo "Republic of the Congo"). With the promulgation of the [Luluabourg Constitution](/wiki/Luluabourg_Constitution "Luluabourg Constitution") on 1 August 1964, the country became the DRC but was renamed [Zaire](/wiki/Zaire "Zaire") (a past name for the Congo River) on 27 October 1971 by President [Mobutu Sese Seko](/wiki/Mobutu_Sese_Seko "Mobutu Sese Seko") as part of his *[Authenticité](/wiki/Authenticit%C3%A9_(Zaire) "Authenticité (Zaire)")* initiative.[[34]](#cite_note-kisanganibobb-38)

The word *Zaire* is from a [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") adaptation of a Kikongo word *nzadi* ("river"), a truncation of *nzadi o nzere* ("river swallowing rivers").[[35]](#cite_note-39)[[36]](#cite_note-40)[[37]](#cite_note-41) The river was known as *Zaire* during the 16th and 17th centuries. *Congo* seems to have replaced *Zaire* gradually in English usage during the 18th century, and *Congo* is the preferred English name in 19th-century literature, although references to *Zaire* as the name used by the natives (i.e., derived from Portuguese usage) remained common.[[38]](#cite_note-42)

In 1992, the Sovereign National Conference voted to change the name of the country to the "Democratic Republic of the Congo", but the change was not made.[[39]](#cite_note-43) The country's name was later restored by President [Laurent-Désiré Kabila](/wiki/Laurent-D%C3%A9sir%C3%A9_Kabila "Laurent-Désiré Kabila") when he overthrew Mobutu in 1997.[[40]](#cite_note-44) To distinguish it from the neighboring Republic of the Congo, it is sometimes referred to as *Congo (Kinshasa)*, *Congo-Kinshasa*, or *Big Congo*.[[41]](#cite_note-little_congo-45) Its name is sometimes also abbreviated as *Congo DR*, *DR Congo*,[[42]](#cite_note-DR-Congo-46) *DRC*,[[43]](#cite_note-DRCabb-47) *the DROC*,[[44]](#cite_note-DROC-48) and *RDC* (in French).[[43]](#cite_note-DRCabb-47)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/History_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "History of the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early history")]

Main articles: [Early Congolese history](/wiki/Early_Congolese_history "Early Congolese history") and [Kingdom of Kongo](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kongo "Kingdom of Kongo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/West_Central_Africa_1350CE.png/250px-West_Central_Africa_1350CE.png)](/wiki/File:West_Central_Africa_1350CE.png)

States of the western Congo Basin, c. 1350

The area of the present day Democratic Republic of the Congo was inhabited as early as 90,000 years ago, and later underwent major demographic and technological change with the expansion of [Bantu peoples](/wiki/Bantu_peoples "Bantu peoples") during the first millennium BC.[[45]](#cite_note-49): 17–18 From this process emerged organised states and empires, including early federated states around Pool Malebo and later the [Kingdom of Kongo](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kongo "Kingdom of Kongo"),[[46]](#cite_note-50) as well as the [Luba](/wiki/Luba_Empire "Luba Empire") and [Lunda Empires](/wiki/Lunda_Empire "Lunda Empire").

### Congo Free State (1877–1908)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Congo Free State (1877–1908)")]

From the 1870s to 1908, King [Leopold II](/wiki/Leopold_II_of_Belgium "Leopold II of Belgium") of Belgium established and ruled the [Congo Free State](/wiki/Congo_Free_State "Congo Free State") as his personal possession, using forced labour and concessionary companies to extract rubber, while violence, disease and exploitation caused catastrophic population loss.[[47]](#cite_note-51)[[48]](#cite_note-52)[[49]](#cite_note-53)[[9]](#cite_note-Luneghe_GPJ_I_Will_Not_Leave_This_Place-13) International exposure of the abuses, notably through the 1904 [Casement Report](/wiki/Casement_Report "Casement Report") and growing diplomatic pressure, led to Leopold's loss of control and the annexation of the territory as the Belgian Congo in 1908.

### Belgian Congo (1908–1960)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Belgian Congo (1908–1960)")]

Main article: [Belgian Congo](/wiki/Belgian_Congo "Belgian Congo")

The Belgian Congo was governed directly from [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), and although this change in administration ended the most extreme abuses of the Congo Free State, Belgian rule largely continued earlier patterns of direct control, racial segregation, forced labour, and repression, while prioritising economic exploitation through close cooperation between the state, missionaries, and private companies. After the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), urbanisation, development programmes, and limited social improvements fostered a small African middle class, but political exclusion persisted, contributing to the rise of nationalist movements and escalating demands for independence in the late 1950s.

### Independence and political crisis (1960–1965)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Independence and political crisis (1960–1965)")]

Main articles: [Congo Crisis](/wiki/Congo_Crisis "Congo Crisis") and [Republic of the Congo (Léopoldville)](/wiki/Republic_of_the_Congo_(L%C3%A9opoldville) "Republic of the Congo (Léopoldville)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Joseph_Kasa-Vubu_in_Israel.png/250px-Joseph_Kasa-Vubu_in_Israel.png)](/wiki/File:Joseph_Kasa-Vubu_in_Israel.png)

The leader of [ABAKO](/wiki/ABAKO "ABAKO"), [Joseph Kasa-Vubu](/wiki/Joseph_Kasa-Vubu "Joseph Kasa-Vubu"), first democratically elected [President](/wiki/President_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "President of the Democratic Republic of the Congo") of [Congo-Léopoldville](/wiki/Republic_of_the_Congo_(L%C3%A9opoldville) "Republic of the Congo (Léopoldville)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Patrice_Lumumba_official_portrait.jpg/250px-Patrice_Lumumba_official_portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Patrice_Lumumba_official_portrait.jpg)

[Patrice Lumumba](/wiki/Patrice_Lumumba "Patrice Lumumba"), first democratically elected [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Prime Minister of the Democratic Republic of the Congo") of the [Congo-Léopoldville](/wiki/Republic_of_the_Congo_(L%C3%A9opoldville) "Republic of the Congo (Léopoldville)"), was murdered by Belgian-supported [Katangan separatists](/wiki/State_of_Katanga "State of Katanga") in 1961.

In May 1960, a growing nationalist movement, the [Mouvement National Congolais](/wiki/Mouvement_National_Congolais "Mouvement National Congolais") led by [Patrice Lumumba](/wiki/Patrice_Lumumba "Patrice Lumumba"), won the [parliamentary elections](/wiki/1960_Belgian_Congo_general_election "1960 Belgian Congo general election"). Lumumba became the first [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Prime Minister of the Democratic Republic of the Congo") of the Republic of the Congo, on 24 June 1960. The parliament elected [Joseph Kasa-Vubu](/wiki/Joseph_Kasa-Vubu "Joseph Kasa-Vubu") as [president](/wiki/President_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "President of the Democratic Republic of the Congo") of the Alliance des Bakongo ([ABAKO](/wiki/ABAKO "ABAKO")) party. Other parties that emerged included the [Parti Solidaire Africain](/wiki/Parti_Solidaire_Africain "Parti Solidaire Africain") led by [Antoine Gizenga](/wiki/Antoine_Gizenga "Antoine Gizenga"), and the Parti National du Peuple led by Albert Delvaux and [Laurent Mbariko](/wiki/Laurent_Mbariko "Laurent Mbariko").[[50]](#cite_note-54)

The Belgian Congo achieved independence on 30 June 1960 under the name "République du Congo" ("Republic of Congo" or "Republic of the Congo" in English). Shortly after, on 15 August 1960, the neighboring French colony of [Middle Congo](/wiki/French_Congo "French Congo") also gained independence and adopted the same name, '[Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Republic_of_the_Congo "Republic of the Congo").' To avoid confusion between the two, the former Belgian Congo became known as the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DR Congo), while the former French colony retained the name 'Republic of the Congo' (Congo).

Shortly after independence the *[Force Publique](/wiki/Force_Publique "Force Publique")* mutinied, and on 11 July the [province of Katanga](/wiki/Katanga_Province "Katanga Province") (led by [Moïse Tshombe](/wiki/Mo%C3%AFse_Tshombe "Moïse Tshombe")) and [South Kasai](/wiki/South_Kasai "South Kasai") [engaged in secessionist struggles](/wiki/Congo_Crisis "Congo Crisis") against the new leadership.[[51]](#cite_note-55)[[52]](#cite_note-56) Most of the 100,000 [Europeans](/wiki/White_people_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "White people in the Democratic Republic of the Congo") who had remained behind after independence fled the country.[[53]](#cite_note-57) After the United Nations rejected Lumumba's call for help to put down the secessionist movements, Lumumba asked for assistance from the Soviet Union, who accepted and sent military supplies and advisers. On 23 August, the Congolese armed forces [invaded South Kasai](/wiki/Invasion_of_South_Kasai "Invasion of South Kasai"). Lumumba was dismissed from office on 5 September 1960 by Kasa-Vubu who publicly blamed him for massacres by the armed forces in South Kasai and for involving Soviets in the country.[[54]](#cite_note-58) On 7 September, Lumumba made a speech to the Congolese House of Representatives, arguing his dismissal was illegal under the nation's laws. Congolese law gave parliament, not the president, the authority to dismiss a government minister. The House and Senate both rejected the dismissal of Lumumba, but the removal proceeded unconstitutionally.[[55]](#cite_note-59)

On 14 September, Colonel [Joseph Mobutu](/wiki/Mobutu_Sese_Seko "Mobutu Sese Seko"), with the backing of the US and Belgium, removed Lumumba from office. On 17 January 1961, Lumumba was handed over to Katangan authorities and executed by Belgian-led Katangan troops.[[56]](#cite_note-60) A 2001 investigation by Belgium's Parliament found Belgium "morally responsible" for the murder of Lumumba, and the country has since officially apologised for its role in his death.[[57]](#cite_note-61)

On 18 September 1961, in ongoing negotiations of a ceasefire, a [plane crash near Ndola](/wiki/1961_Ndola_United_Nations_DC-6_crash "1961 Ndola United Nations DC-6 crash") resulted in the death of [Dag Hammarskjöld](/wiki/Dag_Hammarskj%C3%B6ld "Dag Hammarskjöld"), the [Secretary-General of the United Nations](/wiki/Secretary-General_of_the_United_Nations "Secretary-General of the United Nations"), along with all 15 passengers, setting off a succession crisis. Amidst widespread confusion and chaos, a temporary government was led by technicians (the [Collège des commissaires généraux](/wiki/College_of_Commissioners-General "College of Commissioners-General")). Katangan secession ended in January 1963 with the [assistance of UN forces](/wiki/United_Nations_Operation_in_the_Congo "United Nations Operation in the Congo"). Several short-lived governments of [Joseph Ileo](/wiki/Joseph_Il%C3%A9o "Joseph Iléo"), [Cyrille Adoula](/wiki/Cyrille_Adoula "Cyrille Adoula"), and Moise Kapenda Tshombe took over in quick succession.

Meanwhile, in the east of the country, Soviet and Cuban-backed rebels called the [Simbas](/wiki/Simba_rebellion "Simba rebellion") rose up, taking a significant amount of territory and proclaiming a communist "People's Republic of the Congo" in Stanleyville. The Simbas were pushed out of Stanleyville in November 1964 during [Operation Dragon Rouge](/wiki/Operation_Dragon_Rouge "Operation Dragon Rouge"), a military operation conducted by Belgian and American forces to rescue hundreds of hostages. Congolese government forces fully defeated the Simba rebels by November 1965.[[58]](#cite_note-62)

Lumumba had previously appointed Mobutu chief of staff of the new Congo army, [Armée Nationale Congolaise](/wiki/Armed_Forces_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Armed Forces of the Democratic Republic of the Congo").[[59]](#cite_note-63) Taking advantage of the leadership crisis between Kasavubu and Tshombe, Mobutu garnered enough support within the army to launch a coup. A [constitutional referendum](/wiki/1964_Republic_of_the_Congo_(L%C3%A9opoldville)_constitutional_referendum "1964 Republic of the Congo (Léopoldville) constitutional referendum") the year before [Mobutu's coup of 1965](/wiki/Second_Mobutu_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "Second Mobutu coup d'état") resulted in the country's official name being changed to the "Democratic Republic of the Congo".[[2]](#cite_note-cia.gov-3) In 1971 Mobutu changed the name again, this time to "Republic of Zaire".[[60]](#cite_note-britannica-64)[[34]](#cite_note-kisanganibobb-38)

### Mobutu autocracy and Zaire (1965–1997)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Mobutu autocracy and Zaire (1965–1997)")]

Main article: [Zaire](/wiki/Zaire "Zaire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Mobutu_Nixon.jpg/250px-Mobutu_Nixon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mobutu_Nixon.jpg)

[Mobutu Sese Seko](/wiki/Mobutu_Sese_Seko "Mobutu Sese Seko") and Richard Nixon in Washington, D.C., 1973

Mobutu had the staunch support of the United States because of his [opposition to communism](/wiki/Anti-communism "Anti-communism"); the U.S. believed that his administration would serve as an effective counter to communist movements in Africa.[[61]](#cite_note-65) A [single-party system](/wiki/One-party_state "One-party state") was established, and Mobutu declared himself [head of state](/wiki/List_of_heads_of_state_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "List of heads of state of the Democratic Republic of the Congo"). He periodically held elections in which he was the only candidate. Although relative peace and stability were achieved, Mobutu's government was guilty of severe [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights") violations, [political repression](/wiki/Political_repression "Political repression"), a [cult of personality](/wiki/Cult_of_personality "Cult of personality") and [corruption](/wiki/Corruption_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Corruption in the Democratic Republic of the Congo").

By late 1967, Mobutu had successfully neutralized his political opponents and rivals, either through co-opting them into his regime, arresting them, or rendering them otherwise politically impotent.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoungTurner201358-66) Throughout the late 1960s, Mobutu continued to shuffle his governments and cycle officials in and out of the office to maintain control. Joseph Kasa-Vubu's death in April 1969 ensured that no person with First Republic credentials could challenge his rule.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoungTurner201361–62-67) By the early 1970s, Mobutu was attempting to assert Zaire as a leading African nation. He traveled frequently across the continent while the government became more vocal about African issues, particularly those relating to the southern region. Zaire established semi-clientelist relationships with several smaller African states, especially Burundi, Chad, and Togo.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoungTurner201364-68)

Corruption became so common the term "le mal Zairois" or "Zairian sickness",[[65]](#cite_note-69) meaning gross corruption, theft and mismanagement, was coined, reportedly by Mobutu.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoungTurner201374-70) International aid, most often in the form of loans, enriched Mobutu while he allowed national infrastructure such as roads to deteriorate to as little as one-quarter of what had existed in 1960. Zaire became a [kleptocracy](/wiki/Kleptocracy "Kleptocracy") as Mobutu and his associates embezzled government funds.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Prince_Bernhard_and_Mobutu_Sese_Seko_1973.jpg/250px-Prince_Bernhard_and_Mobutu_Sese_Seko_1973.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prince_Bernhard_and_Mobutu_Sese_Seko_1973.jpg)

Mobutu with the Dutch [Prince Bernhard](/wiki/Bernhard_of_Lippe-Biesterfeld "Bernhard of Lippe-Biesterfeld") in Kinshasa in 1973

In a campaign to identify himself with African nationalism, starting on 1 June 1966, Mobutu renamed the nation's cities: Léopoldville became Kinshasa (the country was known as Congo-Kinshasa), Stanleyville became [Kisangani](/wiki/Kisangani "Kisangani"), Elisabethville became [Lubumbashi](/wiki/Lubumbashi "Lubumbashi"), and Coquilhatville became [Mbandaka](/wiki/Mbandaka "Mbandaka"). In 1971, Mobutu renamed the country the *Republic of Zaire*,[[34]](#cite_note-kisanganibobb-38) its fourth name change in eleven years and its sixth overall. The Congo River was renamed the Zaire River.

During the 1970s and 1980s, Mobutu was invited to visit the United States on several occasions, meeting with U.S. Presidents [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon"), [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") and [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush").[[67]](#cite_note-71) Following the [dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union") U.S. relations with Mobutu cooled, as he was no longer deemed necessary as a [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") ally. Opponents within Zaire stepped up demands for reform. This atmosphere contributed to Mobutu's declaring the Third Republic in 1990, whose constitution was supposed to pave the way for democratic reform. The reforms turned out to be largely cosmetic. Mobutu continued in power until armed forces forced him to flee in 1997. "From 1990 to 1993, the United States facilitated Mobutu's attempts to hijack political change", one academic wrote, and "also assisted the rebellion of Laurent-Desire Kabila that overthrew the Mobutu regime."[[68]](#cite_note-72)

In September 1997, Mobutu died in exile in Morocco.[[69]](#cite_note-73)

### Continental and civil wars (1996–2007)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Continental and civil wars (1996–2007)")]

Main articles: [First Congo War](/wiki/First_Congo_War "First Congo War") and [Second Congo War](/wiki/Second_Congo_War "Second Congo War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Second_Congo_War_Africa_map_en.png/250px-Second_Congo_War_Africa_map_en.png)](/wiki/File:Second_Congo_War_Africa_map_en.png)

Belligerents of the [Second Congo War](/wiki/Second_Congo_War "Second Congo War")

By 1996, following the [Rwandan Civil War](/wiki/Rwandan_Civil_War "Rwandan Civil War") and [genocide](/wiki/Rwandan_genocide "Rwandan genocide") and the ascension of a [Tutsi](/wiki/Tutsi "Tutsi")-led government in Rwanda, Rwandan [Hutu](/wiki/Hutu "Hutu") militia forces ([Interahamwe](/wiki/Interahamwe "Interahamwe")) fled to eastern Zaire and used refugee camps as bases for incursions against Rwanda. They allied with the Zairian Armed Forces to launch a campaign against Congolese ethnic Tutsis in eastern Zaire.[[70]](#cite_note-74)

A coalition of Rwandan and Ugandan armies invaded Zaire to overthrow the government of Mobutu, launching the First Congo War. The coalition allied with some opposition figures, led by [Laurent-Désiré Kabila](/wiki/Laurent-D%C3%A9sir%C3%A9_Kabila "Laurent-Désiré Kabila"), becoming the [Alliance of Democratic Forces for the Liberation of the Congo](/wiki/Alliance_of_Democratic_Forces_for_the_Liberation_of_the_Congo "Alliance of Democratic Forces for the Liberation of the Congo"). In 1997 Mobutu fled and Kabila marched into Kinshasa, naming himself as president and reverting the name of the country to the Democratic Republic of the Congo.[[71]](#cite_note-75)[[72]](#cite_note-76)

Kabila later requested that foreign military forces return to their own countries. Rwandan troops retreated to [Goma](/wiki/Goma "Goma") and launched a new Tutsi-led rebel military movement called the [Rassemblement Congolais pour la Democratie](/wiki/Rally_for_Congolese_Democracy "Rally for Congolese Democracy") to fight Kabila, while Uganda instigated the creation of a rebel movement called the [Movement for the Liberation of the Congo](/wiki/Movement_for_the_Liberation_of_the_Congo "Movement for the Liberation of the Congo"), led by Congolese warlord [Jean-Pierre Bemba](/wiki/Jean-Pierre_Bemba "Jean-Pierre Bemba").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The two rebel movements, along with Rwandan and Ugandan troops, started the Second Congo War by attacking the DRC army in 1998. Angolan, Zimbabwean, and Namibian militaries entered the hostilities on the side of the government.

Kabila was assassinated in 2001.[[73]](#cite_note-77) His son [Joseph Kabila](/wiki/Joseph_Kabila "Joseph Kabila") succeeded him[[74]](#cite_note-78) and called for multilateral peace talks. UN peacekeepers, MONUC, now known as [MONUSCO](/wiki/MONUSCO "MONUSCO"), arrived in April 2001. In 2002–03, Bemba intervened in the Central African Republic on behalf of its former president, [Ange-Félix Patassé](/wiki/Ange-F%C3%A9lix_Patass%C3%A9 "Ange-Félix Patassé").[[75]](#cite_note-79) Talks led to a peace accord under which Kabila would share power with former rebels, implemented between 2002 and 2003.[[14]](#cite_note-CFR2025-18) The most widely reported estimate for the death toll during the Second Congo War is 5.4 million people, though other estimates put it at three million. It has been described as the deadliest conflict since [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[14]](#cite_note-CFR2025-18)[[76]](#cite_note-80) By June 2003 all foreign armies except those of Rwanda had pulled out of Congo. A [transitional government](/wiki/Transitional_Government_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Transitional Government of the Democratic Republic of the Congo") was set up until an election was held. A constitution was approved by voters; further, on 30 July 2006, DRC held its [first multi-party elections](/wiki/2006_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_general_election "2006 Democratic Republic of the Congo general election"). These were the first free national elections since 1960, which many believed would mark the end to violence in the region.[[77]](#cite_note-Oxford_University_Press-81) However, an election-result dispute between Kabila and Bemba turned into a skirmish between their supporters in Kinshasa. MONUC took control of the city. The second round of the election took place in October 2006, which Kabila won, and in December 2006 he was sworn in as president.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Refugees_in_Congo.jpeg/250px-Refugees_in_Congo.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Refugees_in_Congo.jpeg)

Refugees in the Congo

### Continued conflicts (2008–2018)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Continued conflicts (2008–2018)")]

Main articles: [Kivu conflict](/wiki/Kivu_conflict "Kivu conflict"), [Ituri conflict](/wiki/Ituri_conflict "Ituri conflict"), and [2018 Yumbi violence](/wiki/2018_Yumbi_violence "2018 Yumbi violence")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Opening_ceremony_of_new_PNC_headquarters_in_Goma_%286988913212%29.jpg/250px-Opening_ceremony_of_new_PNC_headquarters_in_Goma_%286988913212%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Opening_ceremony_of_new_PNC_headquarters_in_Goma_(6988913212).jpg)

People fleeing their villages due to fighting between [FARDC](/wiki/Military_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Military of the Democratic Republic of the Congo") and rebel groups, [North Kivu](/wiki/North_Kivu "North Kivu"), 2012

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/FARDC_and_MONUSCO_reinforce_their_presence_in_and_around_Goma_following_a_second_day_%2821_May_2013%29_of_clashes_between_M23_and_National_troops._%288782972992%29.jpg/250px-FARDC_and_MONUSCO_reinforce_their_presence_in_and_around_Goma_following_a_second_day_%2821_May_2013%29_of_clashes_between_M23_and_National_troops._%288782972992%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:FARDC_and_MONUSCO_reinforce_their_presence_in_and_around_Goma_following_a_second_day_(21_May_2013)_of_clashes_between_M23_and_National_troops._(8782972992).jpg)

Government troops near [Goma](/wiki/Goma "Goma") during the [M23 rebellion](/wiki/M23_rebellion_(2012%E2%80%932013) "M23 rebellion (2012–2013)") in May 2013

[Laurent Nkunda](/wiki/Laurent_Nkunda "Laurent Nkunda"), a member of [Rally for Congolese Democracy–Goma](/wiki/Rally_for_Congolese_Democracy%E2%80%93Goma "Rally for Congolese Democracy–Goma"), defected along with troops loyal to him and formed the [National Congress for the Defence of the People](/wiki/National_Congress_for_the_Defence_of_the_People "National Congress for the Defence of the People") (CNDP), which began an armed rebellion against the government. In March 2009, after a deal between the DRC and Rwanda, Rwandan troops entered the DRC and arrested Nkunda and were allowed to pursue FDLR militants. The CNDP signed a peace treaty with the government in which it agreed to become a political party and to have its soldiers integrated into the national army in exchange for the release of its imprisoned members.[[78]](#cite_note-82) In 2012 [Bosco Ntaganda](/wiki/Bosco_Ntaganda "Bosco Ntaganda"), the leader of the CNDP, and troops loyal to him, [mutinied](/wiki/M23_rebellion_(2012%E2%80%932013) "M23 rebellion (2012–2013)")[[79]](#cite_note-83) and briefly captured the provincial capital of Goma in November 2012.[[80]](#cite_note-84)[[81]](#cite_note-85)

Neighboring countries have been accused of arming rebel groups and using them as proxies to gain control of the resource-rich country.[[82]](#cite_note-86)[[83]](#cite_note-87) In March 2013, the [United Nations Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council") authorized the [United Nations Force Intervention Brigade](/wiki/United_Nations_Force_Intervention_Brigade "United Nations Force Intervention Brigade") to neutralize armed groups.[[84]](#cite_note-UN1-88) In 2013, the [Mai-Mai](/wiki/Mai-Mai "Mai-Mai") created by Laurent Kabila briefly invaded the provincial capital of [Lubumbashi](/wiki/Lubumbashi "Lubumbashi") and 400,000 people are displaced in the province as of 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit).[[85]](#cite_note-89) On and off fighting in the [Ituri conflict](/wiki/Ituri_conflict "Ituri conflict") occurred between the [Nationalist and Integrationist Front](/wiki/Nationalist_and_Integrationist_Front "Nationalist and Integrationist Front") and the [Union of Congolese Patriots](/wiki/Union_of_Congolese_Patriots "Union of Congolese Patriots"). In the northeast, [Joseph Kony](/wiki/Joseph_Kony "Joseph Kony")'s [Lord's Resistance Army](/wiki/Lord%27s_Resistance_Army "Lord's Resistance Army") moved from their original bases in Uganda and South Sudan to DR Congo in 2005 and set up camps in the [Garamba National Park](/wiki/Garamba_National_Park "Garamba National Park").[[86]](#cite_note-90)[[87]](#cite_note-91)

In 2015, [major protests](/wiki/19_January_2015_DRC_protests "19 January 2015 DRC protests") broke out across the country and protesters demanded that Kabila step down as president.[[88]](#cite_note-The_DRC-92) On 27 November 2016 Congolese foreign minister [Raymond Tshibanda](/wiki/Raymond_Tshibanda "Raymond Tshibanda") told the press no elections would be held in 2016: "it has been decided that the voter registration operation will end on July 31, 2017, and that election will take place in April 2018".[[89]](#cite_note-93) [Protests broke out in the country on 20 December](/wiki/December_2016_Congolese_protests "December 2016 Congolese protests") when Kabila's term in office ended. Across the country, dozens of protesters were killed and hundreds were arrested.

Human Rights Watch said in 2017 that Kabila recruited former 23 March Movement fighters to put down country-wide protests over his refusal to step down from office at the end of his term. "M23 fighters patrolled the streets of the Congo's main cities, firing on or arresting protesters or anyone else deemed to be a threat to the president", they said.[[90]](#cite_note-94) Fierce fighting has erupted in Masisi between government forces and a powerful local warlord, General Delta. The United Nations mission in the DRC is its largest and most expensive peacekeeping effort, but it shut down five UN bases near Masisi in 2017, after the U.S. led a push to cut costs.[[91]](#cite_note-95)

### 2018 election and Tshisekedi presidency (2018–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2018 election and Tshisekedi presidency (2018–present)")]

Main article: [M23 campaign (2022–present)](/wiki/M23_campaign_(2022%E2%80%93present) "M23 campaign (2022–present)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/F%C3%A9lix_Tshisekedi_%26_Denis_Sassou-Nguesso.jpg/250px-F%C3%A9lix_Tshisekedi_%26_Denis_Sassou-Nguesso.jpg)](/wiki/File:F%C3%A9lix_Tshisekedi_%26_Denis_Sassou-Nguesso.jpg)

President [Félix Tshisekedi](/wiki/F%C3%A9lix_Tshisekedi "Félix Tshisekedi") with the president of neighbouring [Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Republic_of_the_Congo "Republic of the Congo") [Denis Sassou Nguesso](/wiki/Denis_Sassou_Nguesso "Denis Sassou Nguesso") in 2020; both wear [face masks](/wiki/Face_masks_during_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Face masks during the COVID-19 pandemic") due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").

On 30 December 2018, a [general election](/wiki/2018_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_general_election "2018 Democratic Republic of the Congo general election") was held. The election was originally scheduled for 23 December, but was postponed after a warehouse fire destroyed several electronic voting machines, prompting concerns about potential tampering.[[92]](#cite_note-96) Polling eventually opened on 30 December across most of the country, though voting remained suspended in certain areas—a measure officials said was intended to protect residents from an ongoing Ebola outbreak in eastern provinces.[[93]](#cite_note-globalpressjournal.com-97)

After polls closed, the government imposed an internet blackout in some regions, announcing that service would be restored following the release of results, which had been slated for 6 January.[[93]](#cite_note-globalpressjournal.com-97)

On 10 January 2019, the electoral commission announced opposition candidate [Félix Tshisekedi](/wiki/F%C3%A9lix_Tshisekedi "Félix Tshisekedi") as the winner of the presidential vote,[[94]](#cite_note-98) and he was officially sworn in as president on 24 January.[[95]](#cite_note-inaug-99) However, there were widespread suspicions that the results were rigged and that a deal had been made between Tshisekedi and Kabila,[[96]](#cite_note-100) as professional election polls carried out on election day found that Martin Fayulu, the leading opposition candidate, was expected to take 47% of the vote, compared to 23% for Felix Tshisekedi and 19% for Emmanuel Shadary, Kabila's hand-picked successor.[[93]](#cite_note-globalpressjournal.com-97)[[97]](#cite_note-101)

In August 2019, six months after the inauguration of Félix Tshisekedi, a coalition government was announced.[[98]](#cite_note-102) Tshisekedi succeeded in strengthening his hold on power, gaining the support of almost 400 out of 500 members of the National Assembly, the pro-Kabila speakers of both houses of parliament being forced out. In April 2021, the [new government](/wiki/Lukonde_cabinet "Lukonde cabinet") was formed without the supporters of Kabila.[[99]](#cite_note-103)

A [major measles outbreak](/wiki/2019%E2%80%932020_measles_outbreak_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "2019–2020 measles outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo") in the country left nearly 5,000 dead in 2019.[[100]](#cite_note-104) The [Ebola outbreak](/wiki/2018_%C3%89quateur_province_Ebola_outbreak "2018 Équateur province Ebola outbreak") ended in June 2020, after causing 2,280 deaths over two years.[[101]](#cite_note-105) The [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") also [reached the DRC](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "COVID-19 pandemic in the Democratic Republic of the Congo") in March 2020, with a [vaccination campaign](/wiki/COVID-19_vaccination_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "COVID-19 vaccination in the Democratic Republic of the Congo") beginning on 19 April 2021.[[102]](#cite_note-106)[[103]](#cite_note-107) The [Italian ambassador](/wiki/List_of_ambassadors_of_Italy_to_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "List of ambassadors of Italy to the Democratic Republic of the Congo") to the DRC, [Luca Attanasio](/wiki/Luca_Attanasio "Luca Attanasio"), and his bodyguard were [killed in North Kivu on 22 February 2021](/wiki/Killing_of_Luca_Attanasio "Killing of Luca Attanasio").[[104]](#cite_note-108) On 22 April 2021, meetings between Kenyan President [Uhuru Kenyatta](/wiki/Uhuru_Kenyatta "Uhuru Kenyatta") and Tshisekedi resulted in new agreements increasing international trade and security (counterterrorism, immigration, cyber security, and customs) between the two countries.[[105]](#cite_note-109) In February 2022, [allegations of a coup d'état](/wiki/2022_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat_allegations "2022 Democratic Republic of the Congo coup d'état allegations") in the country led to uncertainty,[[106]](#cite_note-110) but the coup attempt failed.[[107]](#cite_note-111) In December 2023, President Felix Tshisekedi won [reelection](/wiki/2023_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_general_election "2023 Democratic Republic of the Congo general election") with more than 70% of the vote.[[108]](#cite_note-112)

The M23 Movement had a [resurgence in 2022](/wiki/M23_campaign_(2022%E2%80%93present) "M23 campaign (2022–present)") and [received military assistance from Rwanda](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo%E2%80%93Rwanda_conflict_(2022%E2%80%93present) "Democratic Republic of the Congo–Rwanda conflict (2022–present)"), which accused the Congolese government of supporting the [FDLR](/wiki/Democratic_Forces_for_the_Liberation_of_Rwanda "Democratic Forces for the Liberation of Rwanda") Hutu militia. In early 2025, the conflict escalated with the M23 capture of [Goma](/wiki/2025_Goma_offensive "2025 Goma offensive") and [Bukavu](/wiki/2025_Bukavu_offensive "2025 Bukavu offensive"), involving thousands of Rwandan troops. Peace negotiations between Rwanda and the DRC brokered by the United States took place in the spring of 2025, and culminated in the signing of a peace deal on 27 June 2025. The [peace agreement](/wiki/2025_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo%E2%80%93Rwanda_peace_agreement "2025 Democratic Republic of the Congo–Rwanda peace agreement") calls for the withdrawal of Rwandan troops from eastern Congo within 90 days and the end of the DRC's support for the FDLR, along with the creation of a regional economic integration framework for the two countries.[[20]](#cite_note-France24-27062025-24) Despite the treaty, the [United Nations Humans Rights Office (UNHRO)](/wiki/United_Nations_Human_Rights_Office "United Nations Human Rights Office") found that hostilities in the provinces where Goma and Bukavu are located, increased during the period from January to July 2025. UNHRO found that M23, along with the occupying [Rwandan Defense Force (RDF)](/wiki/Rwandan_Defence_Force "Rwandan Defence Force") soldiers allegedly massacred hundreds of civilians in four villages within [Rutshuru](/wiki/Rutshuru "Rutshuru"), a region within [North Kivu](/wiki/North_Kivu "North Kivu") province.[[109]](#cite_note-113) In July 2025, a Declaration of Principles was signed in [Doha](/wiki/Doha "Doha") as a first agreement on shared objectives for a ceasefire, and to limit future violence in the conflict.[[110]](#cite_note-114) In October 2025, former president Joseph Kabila was sentenced to death in absentia for alleged collaboration with the rebel group M23.[[111]](#cite_note-115)

## Geography

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Cg-map.png/250px-Cg-map.png)](/wiki/File:Cg-map.png)

The map of the Democratic Republic of the Congo

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Koppen-Geiger_Map_COD_present.svg/250px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_COD_present.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_COD_present.svg)

Democratic Republic of the Congo map of Köppen climate classification

The DRC is located in central [sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa"), bordered to the northwest by the [Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Republic_of_the_Congo "Republic of the Congo"), to the north by the [Central African Republic](/wiki/Central_African_Republic "Central African Republic"), to the northeast by [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan"), to the east by [Uganda](/wiki/Uganda "Uganda"), [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda "Rwanda") and [Burundi](/wiki/Burundi "Burundi"), and by [Tanzania](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania") (across Lake Tanganyika), to the south and southeast by [Zambia](/wiki/Zambia "Zambia"), to the southwest by [Angola](/wiki/Angola "Angola"), and to the west by the South Atlantic Ocean and the [Cabinda Province exclave of Angola](/wiki/Cabinda_Province "Cabinda Province"). The country lies between latitudes [6°N](/wiki/6th_parallel_north "6th parallel north") and [14°S](/wiki/14th_parallel_south "14th parallel south"), and longitudes [12°E](/wiki/12th_meridian_east "12th meridian east") and [32°E](/wiki/32nd_meridian_east "32nd meridian east"). It straddles the [Equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator"), with one-third to the north and two-thirds to the south. With an area of 2,345,408 square kilometres (905,567 sq mi), it is the second-largest country in Africa by area, after [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria").

As a result of its equatorial location, the DRC experiences high precipitation and has the highest frequency of thunderstorms in the world. The annual rainfall can total upwards of 2,000 millimetres (80 in) in some places, and the area sustains the [Congo rainforest](/wiki/Congolian_rainforests "Congolian rainforests"), the second-largest rainforest in the world after the [Amazon rainforest](/wiki/Amazon_rainforest "Amazon rainforest"). This massive expanse of lush jungle covers most of the vast, low-lying central [basin](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin") of the river, which slopes toward the Atlantic Ocean in the west. This area is surrounded by [plateaus](/wiki/Plateau "Plateau") merging into [savannas](/wiki/Savanna "Savanna") in the south and southwest, by mountainous terraces in the west, and dense [grasslands](/wiki/Grassland "Grassland") extending beyond the Congo River in the north. The glaciated [Rwenzori Mountains](/wiki/Rwenzori_Mountains "Rwenzori Mountains") are found in the extreme eastern region.

The [tropical climate](/wiki/Tropical_climate "Tropical climate") produced the Congo River system which dominates the region topographically along with the rainforest it flows through. The [Congo Basin](/wiki/Congo_Basin "Congo Basin") occupies nearly the entire country and an area of nearly 1,000,000 km2 (390,000 sq mi). The river and its tributaries form the backbone of Congolese economics and transportation. Major tributaries include the [Kasai](/wiki/Kasai_River "Kasai River"), [Sangha](/wiki/Sangha_River "Sangha River"), [Ubangi](/wiki/Ubangi_River "Ubangi River"), [Ruzizi](/wiki/Ruzizi_River "Ruzizi River"), [Aruwimi](/wiki/Aruwimi_River "Aruwimi River"), and [Lulonga](/wiki/Lulonga_River "Lulonga River").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Dem_congo_sat.jpg/250px-Dem_congo_sat.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dem_congo_sat.jpg)

Satellite image of Democratic Republic of the Congo

The Congo River has the second-largest flow and the second-largest [watershed](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin") of any river in the world (trailing the [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon_River "Amazon River") in both respects). The sources of the Congo River are in the [Albertine Rift](/wiki/Albertine_Rift "Albertine Rift") Mountains that flank the western branch of the [East African Rift](/wiki/East_African_Rift "East African Rift"), as well as [Lake Tanganyika](/wiki/Lake_Tanganyika "Lake Tanganyika") and [Lake Mweru](/wiki/Lake_Mweru "Lake Mweru"). The river flows generally west from Kisangani just below [Boyoma Falls](/wiki/Boyoma_Falls "Boyoma Falls"), then gradually bends southwest, passing by [Mbandaka](/wiki/Mbandaka "Mbandaka"), joining with the Ubangi River, and running into the [Pool Malebo](/wiki/Pool_Malebo "Pool Malebo") (Stanley Pool). Kinshasa and [Brazzaville](/wiki/Brazzaville "Brazzaville") are on opposite sides of the river at the Pool. Then the river narrows and falls through a number of cataracts in deep canyons, collectively known as the [Livingstone Falls](/wiki/Livingstone_Falls "Livingstone Falls"), and runs past [Boma](/wiki/Boma,_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Boma, Democratic Republic of the Congo") into the Atlantic Ocean. The river and a 37-kilometre-wide (23 mi) strip of coastline on its north bank provide the country's only outlet to the Atlantic.

The Albertine Rift plays a key role in shaping the Congo's geography. Not only is the northeastern section of the country much more mountainous, but tectonic movement results in [volcanic](/wiki/Volcanism "Volcanism") activity, occasionally with loss of life. The geologic activity in this area also created the [African Great Lakes](/wiki/African_Great_Lakes "African Great Lakes"), four of which lie on the Congo's eastern frontier: [Lake Albert](/wiki/Lake_Albert_(Africa) "Lake Albert (Africa)"), [Lake Kivu](/wiki/Lake_Kivu "Lake Kivu"), [Lake Edward](/wiki/Lake_Edward "Lake Edward"), and Lake Tanganyika.

The [rift valley](/wiki/Rift_valley "Rift valley") has exposed an enormous amount of mineral wealth throughout the south and east of the Congo, making it accessible to mining. [Cobalt](/wiki/Cobalt "Cobalt"), copper, [cadmium](/wiki/Cadmium "Cadmium"), industrial and gem-quality [diamonds](/wiki/Diamonds "Diamonds"), gold, silver, [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc"), [manganese](/wiki/Manganese "Manganese"), [tin](/wiki/Tin "Tin"), [germanium](/wiki/Germanium "Germanium"), [uranium](/wiki/Uranium "Uranium"), [radium](/wiki/Radium "Radium"), [bauxite](/wiki/Bauxite "Bauxite"), iron ore, and coal are all found in plentiful supply, especially in the Congo's southeastern Katanga region.[[112]](#cite_note-116) The gold production in 2015 is 37 metric tonnes.[[113]](#cite_note-117)

On 17 January 2002, [Mount Nyiragongo](/wiki/Mount_Nyiragongo "Mount Nyiragongo") [erupted](/wiki/Volcanic_eruption "Volcanic eruption"), with three streams of extremely fluid [lava](/wiki/Magma "Magma") running out at 64 km/h (40 mph) and 46 m (50 yd) wide. One of the three streams flowed directly through Goma, killing 45 people and leaving 120,000 homeless. Over 400,000 people were evacuated from the city during the eruption. The lava flowed into and poisoned the water of [Lake Kivu](/wiki/Lake_Kivu "Lake Kivu") killing its plants, animals and fish. Only two planes left the local airport because of the possibility of the explosion of stored petrol. The lava flowed through and past the airport, destroying a runway and trapping several parked airplanes. Six months after the event, nearby [Mount Nyamuragira](/wiki/Nyamuragira "Nyamuragira") also erupted. The mountain subsequently erupted again in 2006, and once again in January 2010.[[114]](#cite_note-118)

### Climate change

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Climate change")]

This section is an excerpt from [Geography of the Democratic Republic of the Congo § Climate change](/wiki/Geography_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo#Climate_change "Geography of the Democratic Republic of the Congo").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Geography_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit)]

As of 2023, the DRC is the 18th highest emitting nation of [greenhouse gases](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas") and the highest emitter on the African continent.[[115]](#cite_note-119) The majority of these emissions arise from [land use](/wiki/Land_use "Land use") change and [deforestation](/wiki/Deforestation "Deforestation"), as the country uses little [fossil fuel](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel") and only one-fifth of the population has access to electricity.[[116]](#cite_note-120)[[117]](#cite_note-Geography_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_Gabbatiss-Viglione-121) The DRC is one of the most vulnerable nations to [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") and the least prepared to deal with its impacts.[[118]](#cite_note-122) Climate change in the DRC is predicted to cause more frequent and intense floods, droughts and exacerbated disease epidemics.[[117]](#cite_note-Geography_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_Gabbatiss-Viglione-121) Sectors vulnerable to climate change include agriculture, forestry and energy.[[119]](#cite_note-123)

### Biodiversity and conservation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Biodiversity and conservation")]

Main articles: [Wildlife of the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Wildlife_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Wildlife of the Democratic Republic of the Congo"), [Institut Congolais pour la Conservation de la Nature](/wiki/Institut_Congolais_pour_la_Conservation_de_la_Nature "Institut Congolais pour la Conservation de la Nature"), and [Environmental issues in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Environmental_issues_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Environmental issues in the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

Further information: [List of ecoregions in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/List_of_ecoregions_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "List of ecoregions in the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Mountain_Gorilla_%28Humba_family%29_%E2%80%93_Virunga_National_Park_%2829%29.jpg/250px-Mountain_Gorilla_%28Humba_family%29_%E2%80%93_Virunga_National_Park_%2829%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mountain_Gorilla_(Humba_family)_%E2%80%93_Virunga_National_Park_(29).jpg)

The endangered [mountain gorilla](/wiki/Mountain_gorilla "Mountain gorilla"); half of its population live in the DRC's [Virunga National Park](/wiki/Virunga_National_Park "Virunga National Park"), making the park a critical habitat for those animals.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Okapi2.jpg/250px-Okapi2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Okapi2.jpg)

Found in the [Congolian rainforests](/wiki/Congolian_rainforests "Congolian rainforests"), the [okapi](/wiki/Okapi "Okapi") was unknown to science until 1901.

The rainforests of the Democratic Republic of the Congo contain great [biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity "Biodiversity"), including many rare and [endemic](/wiki/Endemism "Endemism") species, such as the common [chimpanzee](/wiki/Chimpanzee "Chimpanzee") and the [bonobo](/wiki/Bonobo "Bonobo") (or pygmy chimpanzee), the [African forest elephant](/wiki/African_forest_elephant "African forest elephant"), [mountain gorilla](/wiki/Mountain_gorilla "Mountain gorilla"), [okapi](/wiki/Okapi "Okapi"), [forest buffalo](/wiki/Forest_buffalo "Forest buffalo"), [leopard](/wiki/African_leopard "African leopard") and, further south in the country, the [southern white rhinoceros](/wiki/Southern_white_rhinoceros "Southern white rhinoceros"). Five of the country's national parks are listed as [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"): the [Garumba](/wiki/Garamba_National_Park "Garamba National Park"), [Kahuzi-Biega](/wiki/Kahuzi-Bi%C3%A9ga_National_Park "Kahuzi-Biéga National Park"), [Salonga](/wiki/Salonga_National_Park "Salonga National Park") and [Virunga National Parks](/wiki/Virunga_National_Park "Virunga National Park"), and the [Okapi Wildlife Reserve](/wiki/Okapi_Wildlife_Reserve "Okapi Wildlife Reserve"). the Democratic Republic of the Congo is one of 17 [Megadiverse countries](/wiki/Megadiverse_countries "Megadiverse countries") and is the most biodiverse African country.[[120]](#cite_note-124)

Conservationists have particularly worried about [primates](/wiki/Primate "Primate"). The Congo is inhabited by several [great ape](/wiki/Hominidae "Hominidae") species: the common chimpanzee (*Pan troglodytes*), the bonobo (*Pan paniscus*), the [eastern gorilla](/wiki/Eastern_gorilla "Eastern gorilla") (*Gorilla beringei*), and possibly a population of the [western gorilla](/wiki/Western_gorilla "Western gorilla") (*Gorilla gorilla*).[[121]](#cite_note-125) It is the only country in the world in which bonobos are found in the wild. Much concern has been raised about great ape extinction. Because of hunting and habitat destruction, the numbers of chimpanzee, bonobo and gorilla (each of whose populations once numbered in the millions) have now dwindled down to only about 200,000 gorillas, 100,000 chimpanzees and possibly only about 10,000 bonobos.[[122]](#cite_note-gorillas-126)[[123]](#cite_note-chimpanzees-127) The gorillas, chimpanzee, bonobo, and okapi are all classified as [endangered](/wiki/Conservation_status "Conservation status") by the [World Conservation Union](/wiki/International_Union_for_Conservation_of_Nature "International Union for Conservation of Nature").

Major environmental issues in DRC include [deforestation](/wiki/Deforestation "Deforestation"), [poaching](/wiki/Poaching "Poaching"), which threatens wildlife populations, water pollution and mining. From 2015 to 2019, the rate of [deforestation in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Deforestation_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Deforestation in the Democratic Republic of the Congo") doubled.[[124]](#cite_note-128) In 2021, deforestation of the [Congolian rainforests](/wiki/Congolian_rainforests "Congolian rainforests") increased by 5%.[[125]](#cite_note-129)

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

Main articles: [Politics of the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Politics_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Politics of the Democratic Republic of the Congo") and [Elections in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Elections_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Elections in the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

[![Félix Tshisekedi](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/F%C3%A9lix_Tshisekedi_in_2021.jpg/250px-F%C3%A9lix_Tshisekedi_in_2021.jpg)](/wiki/File:F%C3%A9lix_Tshisekedi_in_2021.jpg)

[Félix Tshisekedi](/wiki/F%C3%A9lix_Tshisekedi "Félix Tshisekedi"),  
[president](/wiki/President_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "President of the Democratic Republic of the Congo") since 2019

[![Judith Suminwa](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Judith_Suminwa_Tuluka_2023.jpg/250px-Judith_Suminwa_Tuluka_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Judith_Suminwa_Tuluka_2023.jpg)

[Judith Suminwa](/wiki/Judith_Suminwa "Judith Suminwa"),  
[prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Prime Minister of the Democratic Republic of the Congo") since 2024

The Democratic Republic of the Congo is, constitutionally, a [semi-presidential republic](/wiki/Semi-presidential_republic "Semi-presidential republic")[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECIA_World_Factbook_I"Government"-130) in which the [president](/wiki/President_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "President of the Democratic Republic of the Congo") is the head of state,[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_the_DRCArticle_69-131) elected by [universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage") to a term of five years,[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_the_DRCArticle_70-132) while the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Prime Minister of the Democratic Republic of the Congo") is the head of government[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_the_DRCArticle_90-133) appointed by the president with the support of the majority party or coalition of parties in the [parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Parliament of the Democratic Republic of the Congo").[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_the_DRCArticle_78-134) The prime minister and the [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Cabinet of the Democratic Republic of the Congo"), known as the government, are responsible to the legislature rather than to the president.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_the_DRCArticle_90-133) The parliament is a [bicameral legislature](/wiki/Bicameral_legislature "Bicameral legislature") consisting of the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Senate of the Democratic Republic of the Congo") and the [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "National Assembly of the Democratic Republic of the Congo").[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_the_DRCArticle_90-133) Deputies of the National Assembly are directly elected for a term of five years,[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_the_DRCArticles_101,_103-135) while senators are elected indirectly by provincial legislatures, also for a term of five years.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_the_DRCArticles_104,_105-136) The president is the [commander-in-chief](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief") of the armed forces[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_the_DRCArticle_83-137) and appoints government and military officials.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_the_DRCArticle_81-138) The [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Constitution of the Democratic Republic of the Congo") is the basis for an independent judiciary,[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_the_DRCArticle_149-139) consisting of the [Constitutional Court](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Constitutional Court of the Democratic Republic of the Congo") to evaluate the constitutionality of laws and to settle disputes regarding elections or the separation of powers;[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_the_DRCArticles_160,_161-140) the [Court of Cassation](/wiki/Court_of_Cassation_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Court of Cassation of the Democratic Republic of the Congo") as the [court of last resort](/wiki/Court_of_last_resort "Court of last resort") in the civil and military judicial system;[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_the_DRCArticle_153-141) and the [Council of State](/wiki/Council_of_State_(Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo) "Council of State (Democratic Republic of the Congo)") as the highest administrative court.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_the_DRCArticle_155-142)

The DRC's political system is considered to be [authoritarian](/wiki/Authoritarianism "Authoritarianism") and scores low on rankings of democracy, political rights, and civil liberties. Despite the constitution providing for the separation of executive, legislative, and judicial powers, in practice the president and his associates have dominated the political system.[[139]](#cite_note-BTI-143) [Freedom House](/wiki/Freedom_House "Freedom House") rates the country as "not free" in 2025, citing endemic corruption, the manipulation of the political system, and human rights abuses, including by security forces.[[140]](#cite_note-144) *[The Economist Democracy Index](/wiki/The_Economist_Democracy_Index "The Economist Democracy Index")* described the DRC as an "authoritarian regime" in 2024, ranking it 156 out of the 167 countries that it evaluated.[[141]](#cite_note-145) The DRC was also ranked 163 out of 180 countries in the [Corruption Perceptions Index](/wiki/Corruption_Perceptions_Index "Corruption Perceptions Index") for 2024.[[142]](#cite_note-146) Administrative institutions remain weak, especially in rural areas, undermining the ability of government to provide basic services to the population. The state has also struggled to extend its administration over the country's entire territory.[[139]](#cite_note-BTI-143) The [Fragile States Index](/wiki/Fragile_States_Index "Fragile States Index") ranked the DRC the 5th most fragile country in the world, out of 179 countries, as of 2024.[[143]](#cite_note-147)

During the presidency of [Joseph Kabila](/wiki/Joseph_Kabila "Joseph Kabila") from 2001 to 2019, the government was marked by corruption, a lack of transparency, and human rights violations.[[139]](#cite_note-BTI-143) Kabila remained in office beyond his constitutional mandate, which ended in 2016, by delaying an election.[[144]](#cite_note-JSTOR-2019-148) After it was [held in late 2018](/wiki/2018_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_general_election "2018 Democratic Republic of the Congo general election") Kabila was succeeded by [Félix Tshisekedi](/wiki/F%C3%A9lix_Tshisekedi "Félix Tshisekedi") in the DRC's first [peaceful transition of power](/wiki/Peaceful_transition_of_power "Peaceful transition of power") since independence,[[139]](#cite_note-BTI-143) but the election is widely seen as having been rigged in a deal made between Kabila and Tshisekedi.[[144]](#cite_note-JSTOR-2019-148)[[145]](#cite_note-149) Tshisekedi was [reelected in late 2023](/wiki/2023_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_general_election "2023 Democratic Republic of the Congo general election"), and after negotiations appointed [Judith Suminwa](/wiki/Judith_Suminwa "Judith Suminwa") as prime minister in 2024, the first woman to hold the post.[[146]](#cite_note-WorldBank2024-150)

The current political system is known as the Third Republic, declared by [Laurent-Désiré Kabila](/wiki/Laurent-D%C3%A9sir%C3%A9_Kabila "Laurent-Désiré Kabila") at his presidential inauguration on 29 May 1997.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGondola2002163-151) Apart from changing the country's name from Zaire back to the DR Congo and restoring its previous symbols, Kabila's government made few changes from the dictatorship of [Mobutu Sese Seko](/wiki/Mobutu_Sese_Seko "Mobutu Sese Seko").[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGondola2002163–164-152) After the Second Congo War (1998–2003), during which Laurent-Désiré Kabila was assassinated,[[139]](#cite_note-BTI-143) a transitional constitution was adopted on 4 April 2003[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_the_DRCArticle_228-153) as part of the peace process.[[139]](#cite_note-BTI-143) It was replaced by the current constitution,[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_the_DRCArticle_228-153) the Constitution of the Third Republic, which went into effect on 18 February 2006. The current [flag](/wiki/Flag_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Flag of the Democratic Republic of the Congo") was also adopted.[[150]](#cite_note-154) The [Transitional Government](/wiki/Transitional_Government_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Transitional Government of the Democratic Republic of the Congo"), led by Joseph Kabila, oversaw the creation of the current system: the first cabinet took office in June 2003, the two chambers of the parliament were organized in July 2003, a constitution was adopted by a [referendum](/wiki/2005_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_constitutional_referendum "2005 Democratic Republic of the Congo constitutional referendum") in December 2005, and the country's [first free election](/wiki/2006_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_general_election "2006 Democratic Republic of the Congo general election") in over four decades was held in July 2006.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReyntjens2009261–274-155) Since the process of democratization began in 1990, more than 600 registered political parties emerged in the DRC, though only four of them—the [UDPS](/wiki/Union_for_Democracy_and_Social_Progress_(Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo) "Union for Democracy and Social Progress (Democratic Republic of the Congo)"), [PPRD](/wiki/People%27s_Party_for_Reconstruction_and_Democracy "People's Party for Reconstruction and Democracy"), [UNC](/wiki/Union_for_the_Congolese_Nation "Union for the Congolese Nation"), and [MLC](/wiki/Movement_for_the_Liberation_of_the_Congo "Movement for the Liberation of the Congo")—successfully operate across the entire country.[[139]](#cite_note-BTI-143)

### Administrative divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Administrative divisions")]

Main articles: [Provinces of the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Provinces_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Provinces of the Democratic Republic of the Congo") and [Territories of the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Territories_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Territories of the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

Further information: [Administrative divisions of the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Administrative divisions of the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

The country is divided into the city-province of Kinshasa and 25 [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Provinces of the Democratic Republic of the Congo").[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_the_DRC[httpswwwconstituteprojectorgconstitutionDemocratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_2011s26_Article_2]-156) The provinces are subdivided into 145 [territories](/wiki/Territories_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Territories of the Democratic Republic of the Congo") and 33 [cities](/wiki/Cities_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Cities of the Democratic Republic of the Congo"). Before 2015, the country had 11 divisions.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_the_DRC[httpswwwconstituteprojectorgconstitutionDemocratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_2011s1432_Article_226]-157)[[154]](#cite_note-158)
