### Foreign relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Foreign relations")]

Main article: [Foreign relations of the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Foreign relations of the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Joseph_Kabila_with_Obamas_2014.jpg/250px-Joseph_Kabila_with_Obamas_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joseph_Kabila_with_Obamas_2014.jpg)

President [Joseph Kabila](/wiki/Joseph_Kabila "Joseph Kabila") with U.S. President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") in August 2014

The global growth in demand for scarce raw materials and the industrial surges in China, India, Russia, Brazil and other [developing countries](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country") require that [developed countries](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country") employ new, integrated and responsive strategies for identifying and ensuring, on a continual basis, an adequate supply of strategic and critical materials required for their security needs.[[155]](#cite_note-:8-159) Highlighting the DR Congo's importance to United States national security, the effort to establish an elite Congolese unit is the latest push by the U.S. to professionalize armed forces in this "strategically important" region.[[156]](#cite_note-160)

There are economic and strategic incentives (for external countries) to bring more "security" to the Congo, which is rich in natural resources such as [cobalt](/wiki/Cobalt "Cobalt"), a metal used in many industrial and military applications.[[155]](#cite_note-:8-159) The largest use of cobalt is in [superalloys](/wiki/Superalloy "Superalloy"), used to make [jet engine](/wiki/Jet_engine "Jet engine") parts for high speed war planes. Cobalt is also used in [magnetic alloys](/wiki/Magnetic_alloy "Magnetic alloy") and in cutting and wear-resistant materials such as cemented [carbides](/wiki/Carbide "Carbide"). The [chemical industry](/wiki/Chemical_industry "Chemical industry") consumes significant quantities of cobalt in a variety of applications including [catalysts](/wiki/Catalyst "Catalyst") for petroleum and chemical processing; drying agents for paints and inks; ground coats for porcelain enamels; decolorant for ceramics and glass; and pigments for ceramics, paints, and plastics. The country possesses 80% of the world's cobalt reserves.[[157]](#cite_note-161)

It is thought that due to the importance of cobalt for batteries for electric vehicles and stabilization of electric grids with large proportions of intermittent renewables in the electricity mix, the DRC could become an object of increased geopolitical competition.[[155]](#cite_note-:8-159)

In the 21st century, [Chinese investment](/wiki/China%E2%80%93Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_relations#Present_economic_relations "China–Democratic Republic of the Congo relations") in the DRC and Congolese exports to China have grown rapidly. In July 2019, UN ambassadors of 37 countries, including DRC, have signed a joint letter to the UNHRC defending China's [treatment of Uyghurs](/wiki/Xinjiang_re-education_camps "Xinjiang re-education camps") and other Muslim ethnic minorities.[[158]](#cite_note-162) In 2021, President [Félix Tshisekedi](/wiki/F%C3%A9lix_Tshisekedi "Félix Tshisekedi") called for a review of mining contracts signed with [China](/wiki/China "China") by his predecessor [Joseph Kabila](/wiki/Joseph_Kabila "Joseph Kabila"),[[159]](#cite_note-163) in particular the Sicomines multibillion ['minerals-for-infrastructure' deal](/wiki/China%E2%80%93Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_relations#Sicomines "China–Democratic Republic of the Congo relations").[[160]](#cite_note-164)[[161]](#cite_note-165)

Although located in the Central African [UN subregion](/wiki/United_Nations_geoscheme "United Nations geoscheme"), the nation is also economically and regionally affiliated with Southern Africa as a member of the [Southern African Development Community](/wiki/Southern_African_Development_Community "Southern African Development Community") (SADC).[[162]](#cite_note-166)

### Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Military")]

Main article: [Armed Forces of the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Armed_Forces_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Armed Forces of the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Dungu%2C_Haut-U%C3%A9l%C3%A9_Province%2C_DR_Congo_As_part_of_operation_%22RED_KITE%22%2C_Moroccan_soldiers_of_MONUSCO_train_FARDC_soldiers_%2838702370071%29.jpg/250px-Dungu%2C_Haut-U%C3%A9l%C3%A9_Province%2C_DR_Congo_As_part_of_operation_%22RED_KITE%22%2C_Moroccan_soldiers_of_MONUSCO_train_FARDC_soldiers_%2838702370071%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dungu,_Haut-U%C3%A9l%C3%A9_Province,_DR_Congo_As_part_of_operation_%22RED_KITE%22,_Moroccan_soldiers_of_MONUSCO_train_FARDC_soldiers_(38702370071).jpg)

Congolese soldiers being trained by UN personnel

The military of the Democratic Republic of the Congo, known as the FARDC, consists of the [Land Forces](/wiki/Land_Forces_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Land Forces of the Democratic Republic of the Congo"), the [Air Force](/wiki/Air_Force_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Air Force of the Democratic Republic of the Congo"), and the [Navy](/wiki/Navy_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Navy of the Democratic Republic of the Congo"). There is also a separate [Republican Guard](/wiki/Republican_Guard_(Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo) "Republican Guard (Democratic Republic of the Congo)") that is outside of the FARDC command structure, being directly responsible to the president.[[163]](#cite_note-iiss2023-167)[[164]](#cite_note-ACSS-168) In 2023 there were 103,000 soldiers in the Land Forces, 6,700 in the Navy, 2,550 in the Air Force, 14,000 in the central command, and 8,000 in the Republican Guard. All services together numbered 134,250 personnel, nominally making the FARDC the largest military in Central Africa.[[163]](#cite_note-iiss2023-167) However, the FARDC has been undermined by low levels of professionalism, training, morale, pay, and equipment, along with rampant corruption, and its lack of vehicles and aircraft makes it difficult to move troops across the country's large territory.[[19]](#cite_note-Reuters1-23)[[163]](#cite_note-iiss2023-167)[[164]](#cite_note-ACSS-168)

The FARDC was established in 2003 after the end of the Second Congo War with the integration of many former rebel groups into its ranks,[[164]](#cite_note-ACSS-168) and it still also works with pro-government militias.[[19]](#cite_note-Reuters1-23) Since then it has been a diverse combination of the former Zairian armed forces, rebel groups from the Congo Wars, and other militias that were integrated more recently. President Félix Tshisekedi announced the beginning of military reforms in 2022 to create a more cohesive national army.[[165]](#cite_note-EA22-169) This effort included replacing most of the high command with younger officers that had a record of success and providing more military spending for the years between 2022 and 2024.[[163]](#cite_note-iiss2023-167)[[165]](#cite_note-EA22-169) Despite this, the FARDC continues to be disorganized and has systematic corruption, preventing it from fulfilling its mission to effectively defend the country's entire territory.[[139]](#cite_note-BTI-143)

The armed forces are organized on the basis of geographic joint commands known as defense zones, one each for the western, south-central, and eastern DRC,[[166]](#cite_note-170) which are further divided into military regions.[[163]](#cite_note-iiss2023-167) The Land Forces consist of brigades, and in 2011, those in Eastern Congo were reorganized into regiments. It was estimated in 2023 that in total there are nine brigades, 27 regiments, one artillery battalion, and one military police battalion.[[163]](#cite_note-iiss2023-167) Many units are reported to be at half of their official strength or less because of combat losses and desertions.[[19]](#cite_note-Reuters1-23) The FARDC has spent decades fighting against over 100 armed groups in Eastern Congo and the [Kasaï region](/wiki/Kasa%C3%AF_region "Kasaï region"), including local [Mai-Mai](/wiki/Mai-Mai "Mai-Mai") militias, the Rwandan-backed 23 March Movement (M23), [Nduma Defense of Congo-Renovated](/wiki/Nduma_Defense_of_Congo-Renovated "Nduma Defense of Congo-Renovated") (NDC-R), the [Allied Democratic Forces](/wiki/Allied_Democratic_Forces "Allied Democratic Forces") (which has become part of the [Islamic State](/wiki/Islamic_State "Islamic State")), and the [Lord's Resistance Army](/wiki/Lord%27s_Resistance_Army "Lord's Resistance Army").[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECIA_World_Factbook_I"Military_and_Security"-171)

### Law enforcement, crime, and corruption

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Law enforcement, crime, and corruption")]

Main articles: [Crime in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Crime_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Crime in the Democratic Republic of the Congo") and [Corruption in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Corruption_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Corruption in the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

The [Congolese National Police](/wiki/Congolese_National_Police "Congolese National Police"), known as the PNC, are the primary police force in the Democratic Republic of the Congo.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConstitution_of_the_DRCArticles_182,_183,_184-172)[[169]](#cite_note-173) The DRC has a [civil law system](/wiki/Civil_law_system "Civil law system") primarily based on [Belgian law](/wiki/Belgian_law "Belgian law"), but also customary and tribal law.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECIA_World_Factbook_I"Government"-130) The country accepts the jurisdiction of the [International Criminal Court](/wiki/International_Criminal_Court "International Criminal Court"),[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECIA_World_Factbook_I"Government"-130) having ratified the [Rome Statute](/wiki/Rome_Statute "Rome Statute") on 11 April 2002. The Criminal Code, the Military Criminal Code, and the Code of Criminal Procedure were modified by the DRC's parliament on 31 December 2015 to be in compliance with the Rome Statute.[[170]](#cite_note-174)

A relative of Mobutu explained how the government illicitly collected revenue during his rule: "Mobutu would ask one of us to go to the bank and take out a million. We'd go to an intermediary and tell him to get five million. He would go to the bank with Mobutu's authority and take out ten. Mobutu got one, and we took the other nine."[[171]](#cite_note-a-175) Mobutu institutionalized corruption to prevent political rivals from challenging his control, leading to an [economic collapse in 1996](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo#1990s "Economy of the Democratic Republic of the Congo").[[172]](#cite_note-b-176)

Mobutu allegedly amassed between US$50 million and $125 million during his rule.[[173]](#cite_note-177)[[174]](#cite_note-178) He was not the first corrupt Congolese leader by any means: "Government as a system of organized theft goes back to King Leopold II", noted [Adam Hochschild](/wiki/Adam_Hochschild "Adam Hochschild") in 2009.[[175]](#cite_note-AH-179) In July 2009, a Swiss court determined that the [statute of limitations](/wiki/Statute_of_limitations "Statute of limitations") had run out on an [international asset recovery](/wiki/International_asset_recovery "International asset recovery") case of about $6.7 million of deposits of Mobutu's in a Swiss bank, and therefore the assets should be returned to Mobutu's family.[[176]](#cite_note-180)

President Kabila established the Commission of Repression of Economic Crimes upon his ascension to power in 2001.[[177]](#cite_note-d-181) However, in 2016 the [Enough Project](/wiki/Enough_Project "Enough Project") issued a report claiming that the Congo is run as a violent [kleptocracy](/wiki/Kleptocracy "Kleptocracy").[[178]](#cite_note-182)

In June 2020, a court in the Democratic Republic of the Congo found President Tshisekedi's chief of staff [Vital Kamerhe](/wiki/Vital_Kamerhe "Vital Kamerhe") guilty of corruption. He was sentenced to 20 years' hard labour, after facing charges of embezzling almost $50m (£39m) of public funds. He was the most high-profile figure to be convicted of corruption in the DRC.[[179]](#cite_note-183) However, Kamerhe was released already in December 2021.[[180]](#cite_note-184)

In November 2021, a judicial investigation targeting Kabila and his associates was opened in Kinshasa after revelations of alleged embezzlement of $138 million.[[181]](#cite_note-185)

### Human rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Human rights")]

Main article: [Human rights in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Human_rights_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Human rights in the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

Further information: [Sexual violence in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Sexual_violence_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Sexual violence in the Democratic Republic of the Congo"), [Child soldiers in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Child_soldiers_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Child soldiers in the Democratic Republic of the Congo"), [Military macho-violence in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Military_macho-violence_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Military macho-violence in the Democratic Republic of the Congo"), and [International Criminal Court investigation in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/International_Criminal_Court_investigation_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "International Criminal Court investigation in the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/DRC-_Child_Soldiers.jpg/250px-DRC-_Child_Soldiers.jpg)](/wiki/File:DRC-_Child_Soldiers.jpg)

A group of demobilized [child soldiers](/wiki/Child_soldiers "Child soldiers") in the Democratic Republic of the Congo

The [International Criminal Court investigation in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/International_Criminal_Court_investigation_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "International Criminal Court investigation in the Democratic Republic of the Congo") was initiated by Kabila in April 2004. The International Criminal Court prosecutor opened the case in June 2004. [Child soldiers](/wiki/Children_in_the_military "Children in the military") have been used on a large scale in DRC, and in 2011 it was estimated that 30,000 children were still operating with armed groups.[[182]](#cite_note-186) Instances of child labor and [forced labor](/wiki/Forced_labour "Forced labour") have been observed and reported in the [U.S. Department of Labor](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Labor "United States Department of Labor")'s *Findings on the Worst Forms of Child Labor* in the DRC in 2013[[183]](#cite_note-187) and six goods produced by the country's mining industry appear on the department's December 2014 *[List of Goods Produced by Child Labor or Forced Labor](/wiki/List_of_Goods_Produced_by_Child_Labor_or_Forced_Labor "List of Goods Produced by Child Labor or Forced Labor")*.

The Democratic Republic of the Congo has prohibited [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage") since 2006,[[184]](#cite_note-188) and attitudes towards the [LGBT community](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "LGBT rights in the Democratic Republic of the Congo") are generally negative throughout the nation.[[185]](#cite_note-189)
[Violence against women](/wiki/Violence_against_women "Violence against women") is perceived by large sectors of society to be normal.[[186]](#cite_note-190)
The United Nations [Committee on the Elimination of Discrimination against Women](/wiki/Convention_on_the_Elimination_of_All_Forms_of_Discrimination_Against_Women "Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination Against Women") in 2006 expressed concern that in the post-war transition period, the promotion of women's human rights and gender equality is not seen as a priority.[[187]](#cite_note-191)[[188]](#cite_note-192) Mass rapes, sexual violence and sexual slavery are used as a weapon of war by the Armed Forces of the Democratic Republic of the Congo and armed groups in the eastern part of the country.[[189]](#cite_note-193) The eastern part of the country in particular has been described as the "rape capital of the world" and the prevalence of sexual violence there described as the worst in the world.[[190]](#cite_note-washingtonpost.com-194)[[191]](#cite_note-195) The prevalence of [Female genital mutilation](/wiki/Female_genital_mutilation "Female genital mutilation") is estimated at 5% of women[[192]](#cite_note-196)[[193]](#cite_note-197) and is illegal.[[194]](#cite_note-198)[[195]](#cite_note-199)

## Health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Health")]

See also: [Health in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Health_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Health in the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

In the Kasai province, health authorities declared an ebola disease outbreak with 28 suspected cases as of 4 September 2025.[[196]](#cite_note-200)

the Democratic Republic of the Congo face several issues related to healthcare. Many of this is attributed to factors such as conflicts and political instabilities.[[197]](#cite_note-201)

The result is difficulty in providing transportation for medical treatment and supplies. In addition, financial inequalities prevent access to services as well in North and South Kivu.[[198]](#cite_note-202) Medical supplies and equipment have also been looted from healthcare facilities.[[199]](#cite_note-203)

In the beginning of the 2025 quarter, there was a drop in 50% of children under the age of five years old visiting medical facilities, compared to the same quarter from last year. Also, the number of vaccinated children dropped from 67,000 to 29,000 when comparing the first quarter of 2023 to that of 2025.[[200]](#cite_note-204)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Economy of the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

See also: [Poverty in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Poverty_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Poverty in the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/GDP_per_capita_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_Congo.svg/250px-GDP_per_capita_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_Congo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:GDP_per_capita_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_Congo.svg)

Change in per capita GDP of Congo, 1950–2018. Figures are inflation-adjusted to 2011 [international dollars](/wiki/International_dollar "International dollar").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Democratic_Republic_of_Congo%2C_Trends_in_the_Human_Development_Index_1970-2010.png/250px-Democratic_Republic_of_Congo%2C_Trends_in_the_Human_Development_Index_1970-2010.png)](/wiki/File:Democratic_Republic_of_Congo,_Trends_in_the_Human_Development_Index_1970-2010.png)

DR Congo's [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") scores, 1970–2010

The economy of the DR Congo has grown from US$9.02 billion at the end of the Second Congo War in 2003 to US$72.48 billion in 2024 by [nominal GDP](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)"),[[201]](#cite_note-205) and from [$](/wiki/International_dollar "International dollar")29.23 billion to $190.13 billion by [PPP-adjusted GDP](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP) "List of countries by GDP (PPP)") during the same time period.[[202]](#cite_note-206) Minerals and metal, specifically [cobalt](/wiki/Cobalt "Cobalt") and [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper"), made up 80% of the country's exports in 2023.[[24]](#cite_note-UNCTAD-28)[[203]](#cite_note-WorldBank2023-207) Its largest trade partner by a significant margin is [China](/wiki/China "China"), which represented 41% of its exports in 2024, followed by [Zambia](/wiki/Zambia "Zambia"), [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), and the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates").[[204]](#cite_note-Trade.gov-208) The DRC's economy, especially its mining sector, received significant foreign investment, and after the early 2000s it experienced high GDP growth rates that were above the average for sub-Saharan Africa.[[205]](#cite_note-AfricaRenewal2016-209) This led to improvements in infrastructure,[[205]](#cite_note-AfricaRenewal2016-209) but the growth has not alleviated poverty for the population, the majority of which (65%) is involved in [subsistence agriculture](/wiki/Subsistence_agriculture "Subsistence agriculture").[[203]](#cite_note-WorldBank2023-207) Most jobs are informal and there is also very high youth unemployment.[[203]](#cite_note-WorldBank2023-207) An estimated 73.5% of people in the DRC lived below the [international poverty line](/wiki/International_poverty_line "International poverty line") of US$2.15 per day as of 2024, making the it among the five poorest countries in the world.[[146]](#cite_note-WorldBank2024-150) The DRC had the lowest [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") of the 187 ranked countries through 2011.[[206]](#cite_note-210) Agriculture is also a large sector of the economy,[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECIA_World_Factbook_I"Economy"-29) though its output has declined over the past several decades[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDamaniaBarraBurnoufRuss201617-211) and the country is dependent on food imports.[[203]](#cite_note-WorldBank2023-207) As of 2023 [food-price inflation](/wiki/Food_price_inflation "Food price inflation") had reached 173%.[[208]](#cite_note-:1-212)

At independence in 1960, the DRC was the second-most-industrialized country in Africa after South Africa; it boasted a thriving mining sector and a relatively productive agriculture sector.[[209]](#cite_note-213) It is widely considered one of the world's richest countries in natural resources; its untapped deposits of raw minerals are estimated to be worth in excess of US$24 trillion.[[210]](#cite_note-214)[[211]](#cite_note-215)[[212]](#cite_note-216) Despite such vast mineral wealth, the economy of the DRC has declined drastically since the mid-1980s. The country generated up to 70% of its export revenue from minerals in the 1970s and 1980s and was particularly hit when resource prices deteriorated at that time.[[213]](#cite_note-ExenbergerHartmann2007-217) Due to the fall in commodity prices, widespread corruption, and the collapse of [the currency](/wiki/Zaire_(currency) "Zaire (currency)") from [hyperinflation](/wiki/Hyperinflation "Hyperinflation"), by the early 1990s the formal economy had nearly ceased to exist, with much of the population depending on subsistence agriculture or informal bartering.[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeditzMerrill1994137–138-218) The new government tried to implement a currency reform in 1997 after the First Congo War, but was disrupted by outbreak of the Second Congo War in 1998,[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECIA_World_Factbook_III"Economy"-219) which worsened all of the problems, along with infrastructure breakdown and the lack of a clear legal system. Economic stability improved in 2003 with assistance from international donors following the withdrawal of foreign troops,[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECIA_World_Factbook_II"Economy"-220) though continuing conflicts and the resulting humanitarian crisis still undermine economic development.[[203]](#cite_note-WorldBank2023-207)[[146]](#cite_note-WorldBank2024-150)

### Mining

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Mining")]

Main article: [Mining industry of the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Mining_industry_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Mining industry of the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Diamonds-_Zaire%2C_%28DR_Congo%29_%288458935824%29.jpg/250px-Diamonds-_Zaire%2C_%28DR_Congo%29_%288458935824%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Diamonds-_Zaire,_(DR_Congo)_(8458935824).jpg)

Rough diamonds ≈1 to 1.5 mm in size from the DR Congo

The mining sector has been responsible for much of the DRC's economic growth since the Second Congo War.[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDamaniaBarraBurnoufRuss201617–18-221) The DRC is the world's largest producer of cobalt ore, accounting for 70% of global production in 2023,[[208]](#cite_note-:1-212) and a major producer of copper and [diamonds](/wiki/Diamond "Diamond").[[218]](#cite_note-222) It has 70% of the world's [coltan](/wiki/Coltan "Coltan"), a third of its cobalt, more than 30% of its diamond reserves, and a tenth of its copper.[[219]](#cite_note-223)[[220]](#cite_note-224) In February 2018, global asset management firm [AllianceBernstein](/wiki/AllianceBernstein "AllianceBernstein")[[221]](#cite_note-225) defined the DRC as economically "the Saudi Arabia of the electric vehicle age", because of its cobalt resources, cobalt being essential in the production of the [lithium-ion batteries](/wiki/Lithium-ion_battery "Lithium-ion battery") that power many [electric vehicles](/wiki/Electric_vehicle "Electric vehicle").[[222]](#cite_note-226) Diamonds come from [Kasaï Province](/wiki/Kasa%C3%AF_Province "Kasaï Province") in the central DRC, though by far the largest mines are located in southern [Katanga Province](/wiki/Katanga_Province "Katanga Province") and are highly mechanized, with a capacity of several million tons per year of copper and cobalt ore, and refining capability for metal ore. The DRC is the second-largest diamond-producing nation in the world,[[e]](#cite_note-227) and artisanal and small-scale miners account for most of its production.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Site_Maniema_Mining_Corporation.jpg/250px-Site_Maniema_Mining_Corporation.jpg)](/wiki/File:Site_Maniema_Mining_Corporation.jpg)

A mining site in the [Haut-Katanga Province](/wiki/Haut-Katanga_Province "Haut-Katanga Province")

Smaller-scale economic activity from [artisanal mining](/wiki/Artisanal_mining "Artisanal mining") occurs in the [informal sector](/wiki/Informal_economy "Informal economy") and is not reflected in GDP data.[[223]](#cite_note-228) A third of the DRC's diamonds are believed to be smuggled out of the country, making it difficult to quantify diamond production levels.[[224]](#cite_note-Kitco-229) In 2002, tin was discovered in the east of the country but to date has only been mined on a small scale.[[225]](#cite_note-230) Smuggling of [conflict minerals](/wiki/Conflict_resource "Conflict resource") such as coltan and [cassiterite](/wiki/Cassiterite "Cassiterite"), ores of [tantalum](/wiki/Tantalum "Tantalum") and tin, respectively, helped to fuel the war in the eastern Congo.[[226]](#cite_note-231) Open-pit cobalt mining has led to [deforestation](/wiki/Deforestation_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Deforestation in the Democratic Republic of the Congo") and habitat destruction.[[227]](#cite_note-232)

[Katanga Mining Limited](/wiki/Katanga_Mining "Katanga Mining"), a Swiss-owned company, owns the [Luilu](/w/index.php?title=Luilu&action=edit&redlink=1 "Luilu (page does not exist)") Metallurgical Plant, which has a capacity of 175,000 tonnes of copper and 8,000 tonnes of cobalt per year, making it the largest cobalt refinery in the world. After a major rehabilitation program, the company resumed copper production operations in December 2007 and cobalt production in May 2008.[[228]](#cite_note-233)

During 2007–08, Joseph Kabila's administration entered a 'resources-for-infrastructure' deal with China, creating the [joint venture](/wiki/Joint_venture "Joint venture") [Sicomines](/wiki/Sicomines "Sicomines") (*Sino-Congolais des Mines*), with the majority of the shares owned by the [China Railway Engineering Corporation](/wiki/China_Railway_Engineering_Corporation "China Railway Engineering Corporation") (CREC) while the DRC's [Gécamines](/wiki/G%C3%A9camines "Gécamines") owned the rest. The company received mining rights in exchanged for investing US$3 billion into building infrastructure.[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELandry20189–11-234) Sicomines began production in 2015.[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELandry201814-235) The deal has received criticism for terms that appeared to be disproportionately favorable to China at the expense of the DRC.[[231]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELandry201815–17-236) Félix Tshisekedi's administration ordered an investigation into the deal, which concluded that less than US$1 billion had been spent on infrastructure. Tshisekedi renegotiated the agreement to add new terms, and in 2024 this led to the infrastructure investment being increased to US$7 billion.[[232]](#cite_note-237)

In April 2013, anti-corruption NGOs revealed that Congolese tax authorities had failed to account for $88 million from the mining sector, despite booming production and positive industrial performance. The missing funds date from 2010 and tax bodies should have paid them into the central bank.[[233]](#cite_note-238) Later in 2013, the [Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative](/wiki/Extractive_Industries_Transparency_Initiative "Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative") suspended the country's candidacy for membership due to insufficient reporting, monitoring and independent audits, but in July 2013 the country improved its accounting and transparency practices to the point where the EITI gave the country full membership.

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Main article: [Transport in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Transport_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Transport in the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

Further information: [Rail transport in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Rail transport in the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Matadi_train_station_%2821236934082%29.jpg/250px-Matadi_train_station_%2821236934082%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Matadi_train_station_(21236934082).jpg)

The Matadi railway station, part of the [Matadi–Kinshasa Railway](/wiki/Matadi%E2%80%93Kinshasa_Railway "Matadi–Kinshasa Railway")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Route_Boma_Moanda.jpg/250px-Route_Boma_Moanda.jpg)](/wiki/File:Route_Boma_Moanda.jpg)

Section of National Road 1 between [Boma](/wiki/Boma,_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Boma, Democratic Republic of the Congo") and [Moanda](/wiki/Moanda,_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Moanda, Democratic Republic of the Congo"), Kongo Central

The DRC has 152,373 kilometres (94,680 mi) of roads, out of which only 3,047 kilometres (1,893 mi) are paved. It also has 4,007 kilometres (2,490 mi) of railways, with most being [narrow-gauge](/wiki/Narrow-gauge_railway "Narrow-gauge railway").[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECIA_World_Factbook_I"Transportation"-239) The infrastructure is in a state of disrepair, and the national highway system is very limited; reaching the capital by road is not possible from many parts of the country.[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDamaniaBarraBurnoufRuss201616-240) Since the early 2000s there have been improvements to the road network, but the dense forests and numerous rivers in the DRC make construction and maintenance difficult.[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFosterBenitez201010–11-241) Air and river transportation have an important role due to the terrain and the poor state of the road and rail networks.[[237]](#cite_note-242) Air travel has seen an increase since the early 2000s, with 24 city pairs having airline service as of 2007, although it has a poor safety record.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFosterBenitez201015–16-243) All air carriers certified by the DRC have been banned from European Union airports because of inadequate safety standards.[[239]](#cite_note-244) Despite this, airlines are seen as the most reliable form of domestic travel.[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDamaniaBarraBurnoufRuss201616-240)[[240]](#cite_note-EAC-245) There are eight airlines in the country,[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECIA_World_Factbook_I"Transportation"-239) including the [flag carrier](/wiki/Flag_carrier "Flag carrier") [Congo Airways](/wiki/Congo_Airways "Congo Airways"), and several international airlines service [Kinshasa's international airport](/wiki/N%27djili_Airport "N'djili Airport"). Besides Kinshasa there are three other international airports in the DRC, which are at [Lubumbashi](/wiki/Lubumbashi_International_Airport "Lubumbashi International Airport"), [Kisangani](/wiki/Kisangani_Bangoka_International_Airport "Kisangani Bangoka International Airport"), and [Goma](/wiki/Goma_International_Airport "Goma International Airport").[[240]](#cite_note-EAC-245)

The DRC has about 15,000 kilometres (9,300 mi) of [navigable](/wiki/Navigable "Navigable") [waterways](/wiki/Waterway "Waterway"),[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFosterBenitez201012-246) with the Congo River serving as the spine. Water transport has traditionally been the dominant means of moving around in the DRC and is also used to fill gaps between roads. Around two million tons of cargo pass through the port of Kinshasa on the Congo River every year, more than triple the volume moved by the national railroad company, the [SNCC](/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_nationale_des_chemins_de_fer_du_Congo "Société nationale des chemins de fer du Congo"). River transports are owned by many private operators.[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDamaniaBarraBurnoufRuss201621-247) The country's three economic hubs—Kinshasa in the west, Lubumbashi in the south, and Kinsangani in the northeast—are not connected by roads or rail.[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFosterBenitez20104-248) The rail system is concentrated in the southeast, and Kinshasa is connected by river ferry to [Ilebo](/wiki/Ilebo "Ilebo"), where the rail line to Lubumbashi begins. This line is also critical for the movement of metal and minerals from the southern DRC to ports in Angola or South Africa (via Zambia) to be exported overseas.[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFosterBenitez201012-246)[[244]](#cite_note-249) There is an electrified line between Kinshasa and the Atlantic seaport of Matadi. The track and [rolling stock](/wiki/Rolling_stock "Rolling stock") of the SNCC system is in poor condition, though the more recently built Matadi–Kinshasa line has better track.[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFosterBenitez201012–13-250)[[246]](#cite_note-251)

There are 44 [national roads](/wiki/List_of_roads_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "List of roads in the Democratic Republic of the Congo")[[247]](#cite_note-252) with a total of 58,358 kilometres (36,262 mi),[[240]](#cite_note-EAC-245)[[248]](#cite_note-DE-2024-253) but three of them are considered the most important. [National Road No. 1](/wiki/National_Road_1_(Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo) "National Road 1 (Democratic Republic of the Congo)") (RN1) is the main highway of the road system, connecting seaports in Kongo Central with Kinshasa and cities in the interior, such as Lubumbashi. RN1 reaches the border with Zambia in the south.[[249]](#cite_note-254) [National Road 2](/wiki/National_Road_2_(Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo) "National Road 2 (Democratic Republic of the Congo)") (RN2) connects the central city of Mbuji-Mayi with Goma in the east, with most of it outside of the Kivu region being in a bad condition, and [National Road 3](/wiki/National_Road_3_(Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo) "National Road 3 (Democratic Republic of the Congo)") (RN3) connects Goma to Kisangani, from where a river boat can be taken to Kinshasa.[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDamaniaBarraBurnoufRuss201621-247)[[248]](#cite_note-DE-2024-253)[[250]](#cite_note-255)

### Energy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Energy")]

Main article: [Energy in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Energy_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Energy in the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

Further information: [Renewable energy in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Renewable_energy_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Renewable energy in the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

The generation and distribution of electricity is controlled by [Société nationale d'électricité](/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_Nationale_d%27%C3%89lectricit%C3%A9 "Société Nationale d'Électricité") (SNEL), but only 15% of the country has access to electricity.[[251]](#cite_note-256) The DRC has the infrastructure for hydro-electricity from the Congo River at the [Inga dams](/wiki/Inga_dams "Inga dams"). Both coal and crude oil resources were mainly used domestically up to 2008.[[252]](#cite_note-257) As of 2010, the country had 2,400 [megawatts](/wiki/Megawatt "Megawatt") of [installed capacity](/wiki/Installed_capacity "Installed capacity"), with only 1,000 MW functioning, and the only interconnected part of the power grid is the high voltage transmission line between the Inga dams in Kongo Central and the southern DRC.[[253]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFosterBenitez20104,_8-258) This line runs to the Zambian border and is also used to export electricity to Zambia, Zimbabwe, and South Africa.[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFosterBenitez20106,_8-259) Almost half of companies in the DRC have their own generators due to blackouts and the limitations of the power grid.[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFosterBenitez20108-260) The country has a river system that could provide hydro-electric power to the entire continent, according to a UN report on the country's strategic significance and its potential role as an economic power in central Africa.[[256]](#cite_note-261) The DRC's electricity generation potential from hydro-power is estimated at 100,000 MW.[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFosterBenitez20108-260) The DRC is a member of three electrical power pools. These are Southern African Power Pool, East African Power Pool, and Central African Power Pool.

Because of abundant sunlight, the potential for solar development is very high in the DRC. There are already about 836 solar power systems in the DRC, with a total power of 83 MW, located in [Équateur](/wiki/Province_of_%C3%89quateur "Province of Équateur") (167), Katanga (159), Nord-Kivu (170), the two Kasaï provinces (170), and Bas-Congo (170). Also, the 148 Caritas network system has a total power of 6.31 MW.[[257]](#cite_note-262)

Main article: [Demographics of the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Demographics_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Demographics of the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

Further information: [Poverty in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Poverty_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Poverty in the Democratic Republic of the Congo") and [Child marriage in Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Child_marriage_in_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Child marriage in Democratic Republic of the Congo")

### Population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Population")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_population.svg/250px-Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_population.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_population.svg)

Democratic Republic of the Congo's population between 1960 and 2017

The [CIA World Factbook](/wiki/The_World_Factbook "The World Factbook") estimated the population to be over 115 million as of 2024.[[258]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECIA_World_Factbook_I"People_and_Society"-263) Between 1950 and 2000, the country's population nearly quadrupled from 12.2 million to 46.9 million.[[259]](#cite_note-264) Since 2000, it has maintained a high growth rate of about 3–3.5% per year, growing from 47 million to an estimated 112 million.

### Ethnic groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Ethnic groups")]

Over 250 ethnic groups and 450 tribes (ethnic subgroups) populate the DRC. They are in the [Bantu](/wiki/Bantu_languages "Bantu languages"), [Sudanic](/wiki/Sudanic_languages "Sudanic languages"), [Nilotic](/wiki/Nilotic_languages "Nilotic languages"), [Ubangian](/wiki/Adamawa%E2%80%93Ubangi_languages "Adamawa–Ubangi languages") and [Pygmy](/wiki/Classification_of_Pygmy_languages "Classification of Pygmy languages") linguistic groups. Because of this diversity, there is no dominant ethnic group in the Congo, however the following ethnic groups account for 51.5% of the population:[[15]](#cite_note-:0-19)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/DRC_Ethnic_Groups.png/250px-DRC_Ethnic_Groups.png)](/wiki/File:DRC_Ethnic_Groups.png)

Breakdown of the largest ethnic groups in DRC

* [Luba-Kasaï](/wiki/Luba-Kasai_language "Luba-Kasai language")
* [Kongo](/wiki/Kongo_people "Kongo people")
* [Mongo](/wiki/Mongo_people "Mongo people")
* [Lubakat](/wiki/Luba-Katanga_language "Luba-Katanga language")
* [Lulua](/wiki/Lulua_people "Lulua people")
* [Tetela](/wiki/Tetela_people "Tetela people")
* [Nande](/wiki/Nande_language "Nande language")
* [Ngbandi](/wiki/Ngbandi_people "Ngbandi people")
* [Ngombe](/wiki/Ngombe_language "Ngombe language")
* [Yaka](/wiki/Yaka_people "Yaka people")
* [Ngbaka](/wiki/Ngbaka_languages "Ngbaka languages")

In 2021, the UN estimated the country's population to be 96 million,[[260]](#cite_note-UN_WPP-265)[[261]](#cite_note-UN_WPP_2022-266) a rapid increase from 39.1 million in 1992 despite the ongoing war.[[262]](#cite_note-267) As many as 250 ethnic groups have been identified and named. About 600,000 [Pygmies](/wiki/Congo_Pygmies "Congo Pygmies") live in the DRC.[[263]](#cite_note-268)

### Migration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Migration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Opening_ceremony_of_new_PNC_headquarters_in_Goma_%287134901927%29.jpg/250px-Opening_ceremony_of_new_PNC_headquarters_in_Goma_%287134901927%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Opening_ceremony_of_new_PNC_headquarters_in_Goma_(7134901927).jpg)

Population fleeing their villages due to fighting between FARDC and rebels groups, Sake, North Kivu, 30 April 2012

Given the often unstable situation in the country and the condition of state structures, it is extremely difficult to obtain reliable migration data. However, evidence suggests that DRC continues to be a destination country for immigrants, in spite of recent declines in their numbers. Immigration is very diverse in nature; refugees and asylum-seekers – products of the numerous and violent conflicts in the [Great Lakes Region](/wiki/African_Great_Lakes "African Great Lakes") – constitute an important subset of the population. Additionally, the country's large mine operations attract migrant workers from Africa and beyond. There is also considerable migration for commercial activities from other African countries and the rest of the world, but these movements are not well studied.[[266]](#cite_note-IOMProfile-271) Transit migration towards South Africa and Europe also plays a role.

Immigration to the DRC has decreased steadily over the past two decades, most likely as a result of the armed violence that the country has experienced. According to the [International Organization for Migration](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Migration "International Organization for Migration"), the number of immigrants in the DRC has fallen from just over one million in 1960, to 754,000 in 1990, to 480,000 in 2005, to an estimated 445,000 in 2010. Official figures are unavailable, partly due to the predominance of the [informal economy](/wiki/Informal_economy "Informal economy") in the DRC. Data are also lacking on irregular immigrants, however given neighbouring countries' ethnic links to DRC nationals, irregular migration is assumed to be a significant phenomenon.[[266]](#cite_note-IOMProfile-271)

Figures for Congolese nationals abroad vary greatly depending on the source; sources believe there are between three and six million Congolese living abroad. This discrepancy is due to a lack of official, reliable data. Emigrants from the DRC are above all long-term emigrants, the majority of whom live in Africa and to a lesser extent in Europe; 79.7% and 15.3% respectively, according to estimated 2000 data. New destination countries include South Africa and various points en route to Europe. The DRC has produced a considerable number of [refugees](/wiki/Refugees "Refugees") and [asylum-seekers](/wiki/Asylum-seekers "Asylum-seekers") located in the region and beyond. These numbers peaked in 2004 when, according to [UNHCR](/wiki/UNHCR "UNHCR"), there were more than 460,000 refugees from the DRC; in 2008, Congolese refugees numbered 367,995 in total, 68% of whom were living in other African countries.[[266]](#cite_note-IOMProfile-271)

Since 2003, more than 400,000 Congolese migrants have been expelled from [Angola](/wiki/Angola "Angola").[[267]](#cite_note-272)

[Europeans](/wiki/White_people_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "White people in the Democratic Republic of the Congo") and Asians makeup a significant part of the Democratic Republic of the Congo's migrant population. Most Europeans and Asians went to the country for temporary employment.[[268]](#cite_note-273)

#### Forced displacement and refugees

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Forced displacement and refugees")]

Main articles: [Internally displaced persons in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Internally_displaced_persons_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Internally displaced persons in the Democratic Republic of the Congo") and [Refugees of the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Refugees_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Refugees of the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

the Democratic Republic of the Congo has around 6.9 million people displaced within its borders, more than five million of whom are in the eastern provinces of North Kivu, South Kivu and Ituri. In addition, over 990,000 Congolese refugees and asylum seekers live elsewhere on the African continent.[[269]](#cite_note-274)

### Languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Languages")]

Main article: [Languages of the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Languages_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Languages of the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Variantes_du_fran%C3%A7ais_originaires_de_Belgique_utilis%C3%A9es_en_Afrique.jpg/250px-Variantes_du_fran%C3%A7ais_originaires_de_Belgique_utilis%C3%A9es_en_Afrique.jpg)](/wiki/File:Variantes_du_fran%C3%A7ais_originaires_de_Belgique_utilis%C3%A9es_en_Afrique.jpg)

The Democratic Republic of the Congo speaks a variant of [Belgian French](/wiki/Belgian_French "Belgian French") (dark red), compared to other parts of Africa.

[French](/wiki/French_language "French language") is the [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") of the Democratic Republic of the Congo.[[270]](#cite_note-275) It is culturally accepted as the *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")*, facilitating communication among the many different ethnic groups of the Congo. According to a 2018 [OIF](/wiki/Organisation_internationale_de_la_Francophonie "Organisation internationale de la Francophonie") report, 49 million Congolese people (51% of the population) could read and write French.[[271]](#cite_note-276) A 2021 survey found that 74% of the population could speak French, making it the most widely spoken language in the country.[[272]](#cite_note-lingala-277)

In Kinshasa, 67% of the population in 2014 could read and write French, and 68.5% could speak and understand it.[[273]](#cite_note-278)

In 2024, there were around 12 million native French speakers in the country.[[274]](#cite_note-279)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Map_-_DR_Congo%2C_major_languages.svg/250px-Map_-_DR_Congo%2C_major_languages.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_-_DR_Congo,_major_languages.svg)

The four [Bantu languages](/wiki/Bantu_languages "Bantu languages") with elevated "national" status

Approximately 242 languages are spoken in the country, of which four have the status of national languages: [Kituba](/wiki/Kituba_language "Kituba language") (Kikongo), [Lingala](/wiki/Lingala "Lingala"), [Tshiluba](/wiki/Luba-Kasai_language "Luba-Kasai language"), and [Swahili](/wiki/Swahili_language "Swahili language") ([Congo Swahili](/wiki/Congo_Swahili "Congo Swahili")). Although some limited number of people speak these as first languages, most of the population speak them as a second language, after the native language of their own ethnic group. Lingala was the official language of the *Force Publique* under Belgian colonial rule and remains to this day the predominant language of the armed forces. Since the recent rebellions, a good part of the army in the east also uses Swahili, where it competes to be the regional *lingua franca*.

Under [Belgian rule](/wiki/Belgian_colonial_empire "Belgian colonial empire"), the Belgians instituted the teaching and use of the four Bantu languages in primary schools, making it one of the few African nations to have literacy in local languages during the European colonial period. This trend was reversed after independence, when French became the sole language of education at all levels.[[275]](#cite_note-lang_educ-280) Since 1975, the four national languages have been reintroduced in the first two years of primary education, with French becoming the sole language of education from the third year onward, but in practice many primary schools in urban areas solely use French from the first year of school onward.[[275]](#cite_note-lang_educ-280)

[Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") is taught in public schools as a foreign language due to the [Angolan](/wiki/Angola "Angola"), and to a lesser extent [Mozambican](/wiki/Mozambique "Mozambique") immigrant and refugee communities. Brazil has also been promoting the language in Africa.[[276]](#cite_note-281) The [lexical similarity](/wiki/Lexical_similarity "Lexical similarity") and comparable phonology of French to Portuguese makes it a relatively easy language for people to learn. Many of the roughly 175,000 Portuguese speakers in the DRC are Angolans who fled violence during the [Angolan War of Independence](/wiki/Angolan_War_of_Independence "Angolan War of Independence") and [Angolan Civil War](/wiki/Angolan_Civil_War "Angolan Civil War") of the late 20th century.[[277]](#cite_note-282) Most of them have been repatriated back since the war ended in 2002.[[278]](#cite_note-283)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Religion")]

Main article: [Religion in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Religion_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Religion in the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Lubumbashi_Cathedral.jpg/250px-Lubumbashi_Cathedral.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lubumbashi_Cathedral.jpg)

[The Sts. Peter and Paul Cathedral](/wiki/Sts._Peter_and_Paul_Cathedral,_Lubumbashi "Sts. Peter and Paul Cathedral, Lubumbashi"), [Lubumbashi](/wiki/Lubumbashi "Lubumbashi")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Cathedrale_notre_dame_de_la_victoire_bukavu.png/250px-Cathedrale_notre_dame_de_la_victoire_bukavu.png)](/wiki/File:Cathedrale_notre_dame_de_la_victoire_bukavu.png)

[Our Lady of Peace Cathedral](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Peace_Cathedral,_Bukavu "Our Lady of Peace Cathedral, Bukavu") in [Bukavu](/wiki/Bukavu "Bukavu")

Christianity is the predominant religion of the DRC. A 2013–14 survey, conducted by the [Demographic and Health Surveys](/wiki/Demographic_and_Health_Surveys "Demographic and Health Surveys") Program in 2013–2014 indicated that Christians constituted 93.7% of the population (with Catholics making up 29.7%, Protestants 26.8%, and other Christians 37.2%). A new Christian religious movement, [Kimbanguism](/wiki/Kimbanguism "Kimbanguism"), had the adherence of 2.8%, while Muslims made up 1%.[[279]](#cite_note-DHS_2013-14-284) Other recent estimates have found Christianity the majority religion, followed by 95.8% of the population according to a 2010 [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center")[[280]](#cite_note-religion-285) estimate, while the [CIA World Factbook](/wiki/CIA_World_Factbook "CIA World Factbook") reports this figure to be 95.9%.[[258]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECIA_World_Factbook_I"People_and_Society"-263) The proportion of followers of Islam is variously estimated from 1%[[281]](#cite_note-286) to 12%.[[282]](#cite_note-287)

There are about 35 million Catholics in the country[[2]](#cite_note-cia.gov-3) with six [archdioceses](/wiki/Archdiocese "Archdiocese") and 41 [dioceses](/wiki/Dioceses "Dioceses").[[283]](#cite_note-288) The impact of the Catholic Church is difficult to overestimate. Schatzberg has called it the country's "only truly national institution apart from the state".[[284]](#cite_note-289) Its schools have educated over 60% of the nation's primary school students and more than 40% of its secondary students. The church owns and manages an extensive network of hospitals, schools, and clinics, as well as many diocesan economic enterprises, including farms, ranches, stores, and artisans' shops.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Sixty-two Protestant denominations are federated under the umbrella of the [Church of Christ in the Congo](/wiki/Church_of_Christ_in_the_Congo "Church of Christ in the Congo"). It is often referred to as *the Protestant Church*, since it covers most of the DRC Protestants. With more than 25 million members, it constitutes [one of the largest Protestant bodies](/wiki/List_of_the_largest_Protestant_denominations "List of the largest Protestant denominations") in the world.

Kimbanguism was seen as a threat to the colonial regime and was banned by the Belgians. Kimbanguism, officially "the church of Christ on Earth by the prophet Simon Kimbangu", has about three million members,[[285]](#cite_note-Adherents-290) primarily among the Bakongo of [Kongo Central](/wiki/Kongo_Central "Kongo Central") and Kinshasa.

Islam has been present in the Democratic Republic of the Congo since the 18th century, brought by Arab traders. However, most Muslims are native to the land. Today, Muslims constitute approximately 1% of the Congolese population according to the Pew Research Center. The majority are [Sunni](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam") Muslims.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Paroisse_St_Esprit.jpg/250px-Paroisse_St_Esprit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paroisse_St_Esprit.jpg)

[Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Catholic Church in the Democratic Republic of the Congo") in the Democratic Republic of the Congo

The first members of the [Baháʼí Faith](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith") to live in the country came from Uganda in 1953. Four years later, the first local administrative council was elected. In 1970, the National Spiritual Assembly (national administrative council) was first elected. Though the religion was banned in the 1970s and 1980s, due to misrepresentations of foreign governments, the ban was lifted by the end of the 1980s. In 2012, plans were announced to build a national [Baháʼí House of Worship](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_House_of_Worship "Baháʼí House of Worship") in the country.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Traditional religions embody such concepts as [monotheism](/wiki/Monotheism "Monotheism"), [animism](/wiki/Animism "Animism"), [vitalism](/wiki/Vitalism "Vitalism"), [spirit](/wiki/Spirit_worship "Spirit worship") and [ancestor worship](/wiki/Ancestor_worship "Ancestor worship"), [witchcraft](/wiki/Witchcraft "Witchcraft"), and sorcery and vary widely among ethnic groups. The syncretic sects often merge elements of Christianity with traditional beliefs and rituals and are not recognized by mainstream churches as part of Christianity. New variants of ancient beliefs have become widespread, led by US-inspired Pentecostal churches which have been in the forefront of witchcraft accusations, particularly against children and the elderly.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*][[286]](#cite_note-291) Children accused of witchcraft are sent away from homes and family, often to live on the street, which can lead to physical violence against these children.[[287]](#cite_note-292)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*][[288]](#cite_note-293) There are charities supporting street children such as the [Congo Children Trust](/wiki/Congo_Children_Trust "Congo Children Trust").[[289]](#cite_note-294) The Congo Children Trust's flagship project is Kimbilio,[[290]](#cite_note-295) which works to reunite street children in [Lubumbashi](/wiki/Lubumbashi "Lubumbashi"). The usual term for these children is *enfants sorciers* (child witches) or *enfants dits sorciers* (children accused of witchcraft). Non-denominational church organizations have been formed to capitalize on this belief by charging exorbitant fees for [exorcisms](/wiki/Exorcisms "Exorcisms"). Though recently outlawed, children have been subjected in these exorcisms to often-violent abuse at the hands of self-proclaimed prophets and priests.[[291]](#cite_note-296)

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [Education in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Education_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Education in the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/DRC_classroom.jpg/250px-DRC_classroom.jpg)](/wiki/File:DRC_classroom.jpg)

A classroom in the Democratic Republic of the Congo

In 2014, the [literacy](/wiki/Literacy "Literacy") rate for the population between the ages of 15 and 49 was estimated to be 75.9% (88.1% male and 63.8% female) according to a [DHS](/wiki/Demographic_and_Health_Surveys "Demographic and Health Surveys") nationwide survey.[[292]](#cite_note-297) The education system is governed by three government ministries: the *Ministère de l'Enseignement Primaire, Secondaire et Professionnel (MEPSP*), the *Ministère de l'Enseignement Supérieur et Universitaire (MESU)* and the *Ministère des Affaires Sociales (MAS)*. Primary education is neither free nor compulsory,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] even though the Congolese constitution says it should be (Article 43 of the 2005 Congolese Constitution).[[293]](#cite_note-298)

As a result of the [First](/wiki/First_Congo_War "First Congo War") and [Second Congo Wars](/wiki/Second_Congo_War "Second Congo War") in the late 1990s—early 2000s, over 5.2 million children in the country did not receive any education.[[294]](#cite_note-ilab-299) Since the end of the civil war, the situation has improved tremendously, with the number of children enrolled in primary schools rising from 5.5 million in 2002 to 16.8 million in 2018, and the number of children enrolled in secondary schools rising from 2.8 million in 2007 to 4.6 million in 2015 according to [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO").[[295]](#cite_note-300)

Actual school attendance has also improved greatly in recent years, with primary school net attendance estimated to be 82.4% in 2014 (82.4% of children ages 6–11 attended school; 83.4% for boys, 80.6% for girls).[[296]](#cite_note-301)

### Health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Health")]

Main article: [Health in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Health_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Health in the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

See also: [HIV/AIDS in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/HIV/AIDS_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "HIV/AIDS in the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Life_expectancy_in_DR_Congo.svg/250px-Life_expectancy_in_DR_Congo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Life_expectancy_in_DR_Congo.svg)

Development of life expectancy in the Democratic Republic of the Congo

The [hospitals in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/List_of_hospitals_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "List of hospitals in the Democratic Republic of the Congo") (DRC) include the [General Hospital of Kinshasa](/wiki/Kinshasa_General_Hospital "Kinshasa General Hospital"). The DRC has the world's second-highest rate of [infant mortality](/wiki/Infant_mortality "Infant mortality") (after Chad). In April 2011, through aid from [Global Alliance for Vaccines](/wiki/GAVI "GAVI"), a new vaccine to prevent [pneumococcal disease](/wiki/Streptococcus_pneumoniae "Streptococcus pneumoniae") was introduced around Kinshasa.[[297]](#cite_note-302) In 2012, it was estimated that about 1.1% of adults aged 15–49 were living with [HIV/AIDS](/wiki/HIV/AIDS "HIV/AIDS").[[298]](#cite_note-303) [Malaria](/wiki/Malaria "Malaria")[[299]](#cite_note-304)[[300]](#cite_note-305) and [yellow fever](/wiki/Yellow_fever "Yellow fever") are problems.[[301]](#cite_note-306) In May 2019, the death toll from the [Ebola outbreak](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_Kivu_Ebola_epidemic "2018–19 Kivu Ebola epidemic") in DRC surpassed 1,000.[[302]](#cite_note-307)

The incidence of yellow fever-related fatalities in DRC is relatively low. According to the World Health Organization's (WHO) report in 2021, only two individuals died due to yellow fever in DRC.[[303]](#cite_note-308)

According to the [World Bank Group](/wiki/World_Bank_Group "World Bank Group"), in 2016, 26,529 people died on the roads in DRC due to traffic accidents.[[304]](#cite_note-309)

[Maternal health](/wiki/Maternal_health "Maternal health") is poor in DRC. According to 2010 estimates, DRC has the 17th highest [maternal mortality](/wiki/Maternal_death "Maternal death") rate in the world.[[305]](#cite_note-310) According to [UNICEF](/wiki/UNICEF "UNICEF"), 43.5% of children under five are [stunted](/wiki/Stunted_growth "Stunted growth").[[306]](#cite_note-311)

United Nations emergency food relief agency warned that amid the escalating conflict and worsening situation following [COVID-19](/wiki/Coronavirus_disease_2019 "Coronavirus disease 2019") in the DRC, millions of lives were at risk as they could die of hunger. According to the data of the [World Food Programme](/wiki/World_Food_Programme "World Food Programme"), in 2020, four in ten people in Congo lacked food security and about 15.6 million were facing a potential hunger crisis.[[307]](#cite_note-312)

[Air pollution](/wiki/Air_pollution "Air pollution") levels in the Democratic Republic of the Congo are very unhealthy. In 2020, annual average air pollution in the DRC stood at 34.2 μg/m3, which is almost 6.8 times the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") PM2.5 guideline (5 μg/m3: set in September 2021).[[308]](#cite_note-uchicago1-313) These pollution levels are estimated to reduce the [life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy") of an average citizen of the DRC by almost 2.9 years.[[308]](#cite_note-uchicago1-313) Currently, the DRC does not have a national ambient air quality standard.[[309]](#cite_note-314)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Culture")]

Main article: [Culture of the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Culture_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Culture of the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Hemba_male_figure1.jpg/250px-Hemba_male_figure1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hemba_male_figure1.jpg)

A [Hemba](/wiki/Hemba "Hemba") male statue

The culture of the Democratic Republic of the Congo reflects the diversity of its numerous ethnic groups and their differing ways of life throughout the country—from the mouth of the River Congo on the coast, upriver through the rainforest and savanna in its centre, to the more densely populated mountains in the far east.[[310]](#cite_note-315) Since the late 19th century, traditional ways of life have undergone changes brought about by colonialism, the struggle for independence, the stagnation of the Mobutu era, and most recently, the First and Second Congo Wars. Despite these pressures, the customs and cultures of the Congo have retained much of their individuality. The country's 81 million inhabitants (2016) are mainly rural. The 30% who live in urban areas have been the most open to [Western](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture") influences.

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Literature")]

[Congolese authors](/wiki/List_of_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_writers "List of Democratic Republic of the Congo writers") use literature as a way to develop a sense of [national consciousness](/wiki/National_identity "National identity") amongst the people of the DRC.

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Music")]

Main article: [Music of the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Music_of_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Music of the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

Further information: [Congolese rumba](/wiki/Congolese_rumba "Congolese rumba"), [Soukous](/wiki/Soukous "Soukous"), and [Ndombolo](/wiki/Ndombolo "Ndombolo")

Congo has a rich musical heritage, rooted in traditional rhythms.[[311]](#cite_note-316) The earliest known form of popular [partnered dance](/wiki/Partner_dance "Partner dance") music in Congo was *Maringa*, denoting a [Kongolese](/wiki/Kongo_people "Kongo people") dance practised within the former [Kingdom of Loango](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Loango "Kingdom of Loango"), encompassing parts of the present-day Republic of the Congo, southern Gabon and [Cabinda](/wiki/Cabinda_Province "Cabinda Province").[[312]](#cite_note-317) The style gained popularity in the 1920s–1930s, introducing the "bar-dancing" culture in [Léopoldville](/wiki/Kinshasa "Kinshasa") (now Kinshasa), incorporating unique elements like a [bass drum](/wiki/Bass_drum "Bass drum"), a bottle as a [triangle](/wiki/Triangle_(musical_instrument) "Triangle (musical instrument)"), and an [accordion](/wiki/Accordion "Accordion").[[313]](#cite_note-:2-318)[[314]](#cite_note-:3-319)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Fran%C3%A7ois_Luambo_Luanzo_Makiadi_se_produisant_au_Za%C3%AFre_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Fran%C3%A7ois_Luambo_Luanzo_Makiadi_se_produisant_au_Za%C3%AFre_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fran%C3%A7ois_Luambo_Luanzo_Makiadi_se_produisant_au_Za%C3%AFre_(cropped).jpg)

[Franco Luambo](/wiki/Franco_Luambo "Franco Luambo") and his [OK Jazz](/wiki/TPOK_Jazz "TPOK Jazz") orchestra performing live at [Zaire 74](/wiki/Zaire_74 "Zaire 74")

In the 1940s and 1950s, the influence of [Cuban son](/wiki/Son_cubano "Son cubano") bands transformed *Maringa* into "[Congolese rumba](/wiki/Congolese_rumba "Congolese rumba")". Imported records by Sexteto Habanero and Trio Matamoros, often mislabeled as "rumba", played a significant role.[[315]](#cite_note-:22-320) Artists such as Antoine Kasongo, Paul Kamba, [Henri Bowane](/wiki/Henri_Bowane "Henri Bowane"), [Antoine Wendo Kolosoy](/wiki/Wendo_Kolosoy "Wendo Kolosoy"), [Franco Luambo](/wiki/Franco_Luambo "Franco Luambo"), [Le Grand Kallé](/wiki/Le_Grand_Kall%C3%A9 "Le Grand Kallé"), [Vicky Longomba](/wiki/Vicky_Longomba "Vicky Longomba"), [Nico Kasanda](/wiki/Nico_Kasanda "Nico Kasanda"), [Tabu Ley Rochereau](/wiki/Tabu_Ley_Rochereau "Tabu Ley Rochereau"), and [Papa Noël Nedule](/wiki/Papa_No%C3%ABl_Nedule "Papa Noël Nedule") authentically popularized the style and made significant contributions to it in the 1940s and 1950s.[[315]](#cite_note-:22-320)

The 1960s and 1970s saw the emergence of [Zaïko Langa Langa](/wiki/Za%C3%AFko_Langa_Langa "Zaïko Langa Langa"), one of the most innovative groups of the genre, which has crossed generations and is considered part of Congolese heritage and pop culture,[[316]](#cite_note-321) as well as [soukous](/wiki/Soukous "Soukous"), an [urban](/wiki/Urban_contemporary_music "Urban contemporary music") [dance music](/wiki/Dance_music "Dance music") style that evolved from Congolese rumba. Soukous led to diverse offshoots, such as *ekonda saccadé*, reflecting the [Mongo](/wiki/Mongo_people "Mongo people") rhythmic influence, and *mokonyonyon*, emulating [pelvic thrust](/wiki/Pelvic_thrust "Pelvic thrust") dance movements from the [Otetela](/wiki/Tetela_people "Tetela people") ethnic background.[[315]](#cite_note-:22-320) The same soukous, under the guidance of "*[le sapeur](/wiki/La_Sape "La Sape")*", [Papa Wemba](/wiki/Papa_Wemba "Papa Wemba"), have set the tone for a generation of young men always dressed up in exorbitant designer clothes. They came to be known as the fourth generation of Congolese music and mostly come from the former prominent band [Wenge Musica](/wiki/Wenge_Musica "Wenge Musica").[[317]](#cite_note-322)[[318]](#cite_note-323)[[319]](#cite_note-324)[[320]](#cite_note-325)[[321]](#cite_note-326)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Abeti_Masikini%2C_1978.jpg/250px-Abeti_Masikini%2C_1978.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abeti_Masikini,_1978.jpg)

[Abeti Masikini](/wiki/Abeti_Masikini "Abeti Masikini") in 1978

Political and economic challenges under Mobutu prompted a mass exodus of musicians to Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda, Zambia, [Sierra Leone](/wiki/Sierra_Leone "Sierra Leone"), [Liberia](/wiki/Liberia "Liberia"), [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), expanding the spread of Congolese urban music.[[315]](#cite_note-:22-320)[[322]](#cite_note-327)[[323]](#cite_note-328) Notably, the quartet Ry-Co Jazz played a crucial role in globalizing Congolese music, touring [West Africa](/wiki/West_Africa "West Africa"), the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"), and France.[[315]](#cite_note-:22-320) By the 1980s, numerous Congolese musicians were based in Europe, facilitating the global dissemination of their musical prowess. Congolese lead guitarists became a sought-after commodity, attracting bands worldwide eager to infuse a Congolese flavor into their compositions or learn the intricate art of Congolese guitar dexterity.[[315]](#cite_note-:22-320)

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Media")]

Main article: [Mass media in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Mass_media_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Mass media in the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

Newspapers of the DRC include *[L'Avenir](/wiki/L%27Avenir_(Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_newspaper) "L'Avenir (Democratic Republic of the Congo newspaper)")*, *Radion Télévision Mwangaza*, *[La Conscience](/w/index.php?title=La_Conscience&action=edit&redlink=1 "La Conscience (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/La_Conscience "fr:La Conscience")]*, *[L'Observateur](/w/index.php?title=L%27Observateur&action=edit&redlink=1 "L'Observateur (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/L%27Observateur_(RDC) "fr:L'Observateur (RDC)")]*, *Le Phare*, *[Le Potentiel](/wiki/Le_Potentiel "Le Potentiel")*, *Le Soft* and *LeCongolais.CD*,[[324]](#cite_note-329) a web-based daily.[[325]](#cite_note-330) [Radio Télévision Nationale Congolaise](/wiki/Radio-T%C3%A9l%C3%A9vision_nationale_congolaise "Radio-Télévision nationale congolaise") (RTNC) is the national broadcaster of the Democratic Republic of the Congo. RTNC currently broadcasts in [Lingala](/wiki/Lingala_language "Lingala language") and French.

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Sports")]

Further information: [Football in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Football_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Football in the Democratic Republic of the Congo") and [Rugby union in the Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Rugby union in the Democratic Republic of the Congo")

Many sports are played in the DRC, including [football](/wiki/Football_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Football in the Democratic Republic of the Congo"), basketball, baseball, and [rugby](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Rugby union in the Democratic Republic of the Congo"). The sports are played in numerous stadiums throughout the country, including the [Stade Frederic Kibassa Maliba](/wiki/Stade_Frederic_Kibassa_Maliba "Stade Frederic Kibassa Maliba").[[326]](#cite_note-331) As Zaire, they participated in the [1974 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1974_FIFA_World_Cup "1974 FIFA World Cup"), where they would be drawn into a group containing Scotland, Yugoslavia, and Brazil. The team would concede fourteen goals and fail to score, finishing bottom of the group.[[327]](#cite_note-332)

Internationally, the country is especially famous for its professional basketball [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") and football players. [Dikembe Mutombo](/wiki/Dikembe_Mutombo "Dikembe Mutombo") is one of the best African basketball players to ever play the game. Mutombo is well known for humanitarian projects in his home country. [Bismack Biyombo](/wiki/Bismack_Biyombo "Bismack Biyombo"), [Christian Eyenga](/wiki/Christian_Eyenga "Christian Eyenga"), [Jonathan Kuminga](/wiki/Jonathan_Kuminga "Jonathan Kuminga"), and [Emmanuel Mudiay](/wiki/Emmanuel_Mudiay "Emmanuel Mudiay") are others who gained significant international attention in basketball. Several Congolese players and players of Congolese descent—including [Romelu Lukaku](/wiki/Romelu_Lukaku "Romelu Lukaku"), [Yannick Bolasie](/wiki/Yannick_Bolasie "Yannick Bolasie"), [Yoane Wissa](/wiki/Yoane_Wissa "Yoane Wissa"), and [Dieumerci Mbokani](/wiki/Dieumerci_Mbokani "Dieumerci Mbokani")—have gained prominence in world football. DR Congo has twice won the [African Cup of Nations](/wiki/Africa_Cup_of_Nations "Africa Cup of Nations") football tournament.

DR Congo's women's national volleyball team lastly qualified for the [2021 Women's African Nations Volleyball Championship](/wiki/2021_Women%27s_African_Nations_Volleyball_Championship "2021 Women's African Nations Volleyball Championship").[[328]](#cite_note-333) The country featured a national team in [beach volleyball](/wiki/Beach_volleyball "Beach volleyball") that competed at the [2018–2020 CAVB Beach Volleyball Continental Cup](/wiki/2018%E2%80%932020_CAVB_Beach_Volleyball_Continental_Cup "2018–2020 CAVB Beach Volleyball Continental Cup") in both the women's and the men's section.[[329]](#cite_note-334)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** The term "Kikongo" in the Constitution is actually referring to the Kituba language – which is known as *Kikongo ya leta* by its speakers – not the [Kongo language](/wiki/Kongo_language "Kongo language") proper. The confusion arises from the fact that the government of the DRC officially recognizes and refers to the language as "Kikongo".
2. **[^](#cite_ref-8)** [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *République démocratique du Congo, RDC*, pronounced [[ʁepyblik demɔkʁatik dy kɔŋɡo]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French")
3. **[^](#cite_ref-9)** "Congo" ambiguously also refers to the neighbouring [Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Republic_of_the_Congo "Republic of the Congo").
4. **[^](#cite_ref-10)** [Its official name from 1971 to 1997](/wiki/Zaire "Zaire").
5. **[^](#cite_ref-227)** In terms of annual carats produced
